Section E. Advance Payment of Witnesses  
E
.1. Who may authorize 
E.2. Process for advance 
payments 
Travel advances for witnesses may be au thorized in accordance with the PPPM and 
FINCEN SOP. See Subsection 17.B.6. 
A preferred procedure for the expeditious advance of funds for witness travel is to 
provide a travel debit card. The travel debit card program is governed by Government 
Travel Card (GTCC) Program Policies and Procedures, COMDTINST M4600.18 
(series), and is used as a tool to obtain a travel advance when someone does not possess 
a Government Travel Charge Card (GTCC), time does not allow for the use of the 
routine travel advance process, and non- receipt of a travel advance would cause 
financial hardship to the witness. Additional information can be found at the GTCC 
website. https://www.dcms.uscg.mil/Our-Organization/Assistant-Commandant-for-
Human-Resources-CG-1/Personnel-Service-Center-PSC/BOPS/PSC-BOPS-
R/GOVTrvl/Travel_Card/. It is critical that supporting documents by complete and 
accurate in order for FINCEN to reimburse  the bank for the issu ed debit card. The 
advanced fees, allowances, and travel expenses that are represented by the witness’ debit 
card must be included on the witness’ travel claim as a prior payment.

Section F. Evidence of Deposition 
F.1. Certificate of The certificate of a person before whom the witness gave a deposition is evidence of the 
person before whom fact and period of attendance of the witness and the place from which summoned. 
deposition is taken

section: Chapter 18 -- Oaths, Article 39(a) Sessions, and Release of Information
chapter_title: Oaths, Article 39(a) Sessions, and Release of Information
heading_path: Oaths, Article 39(a) Sessions, and Release of Information
text:
Oaths, Article 39(a), UCMJ Sessions, Release of Information, and Spectators

Section A. Administered Oaths

A.1. Military judge A military judge, certified in accordance with Article 26(b), UCMJ, may take a one-time 
oath to perform all duties faithfully and impar tially in all cases to which detailed. This 
oath may be taken at any time and may be administered by any person authorized by 
Article 136, UCMJ, to administer oaths. Once such an oath is taken, the military judge 
need not be sworn again at any court-martial to which subsequently detailed. Military 
judges will customarily be given a one-time oath. In the event that a military judge 
detailed to a particular court-martial has not been previously sworn, the trial counsel will 
administer the oath to the military judge at the appropriate point in the proceedings. The 
following oath is used for the swearing in of military judges, see RCM 807(b)(2) 
discussion: 
Do you swear (or affirm) that you will faithfully and impartially perform, according to 
your conscience and the laws applicable to trials by courts-martial, all the duties 
incumbent upon you as military judge to this court-martial (, so help you God)?

A.2. Counsel Any military counsel, certified in accordance with Article 27(b), UCMJ, may be given 
a one-time oath. Such oath will customarily be administered when military counsel is 
certified. The oath may be given at any time and by any person authorized by Article 
136, UCMJ, to administer oaths. Once such an oath is taken, certified military counsel 
need not be sworn again at any trial to which he or she is detailed, or in any case in 
which he or she is serving as individual counsel at the request of the accused. Certified 
military counsel who have taken one-time oaths administered by armed services other 
than the Coast Guard need not again be sw orn in courts-martial convened in the Coast 
Guard. Counsel who are not certified in accordance with Article 27(b), UCMJ, including 
all civilian counsel, must be sworn in each case. The following oath may be used in 
administering a one-time oath to counsel, see RCM 807(b)(2) discussion: 
Do you swear (or affirm) that you will faithfully perform the duties of counsel in the case 
now in hearing (, so help you God)?

source: Coast Guard Military Justice Manual (COMDTINST M5810.1H)

h Article 27(b), UCMJ, including 
all civilian counsel, must be sworn in each case. The following oath may be used in 
administering a one-time oath to counsel, see RCM 807(b)(2) discussion: 
Do you swear (or affirm) that you will faithfully perform the duties of counsel in the case 
now in hearing (, so help you God)?

A.3. Court members  Court members may be given one oath for all cases that are referred to the court in 
accordance with the convening order that deta iled them as members. In the event the 
convening order is amended, a new member will be sworn when he or she arrives. This 
oath may be administered by any officer authorized by Article 136, UCMJ, to administer 
oaths. When court members are not sworn at tr ial, the fact that they have previously 
been sworn will be stated on the record (i.e. captured on the digital audio file of the 
recording of the court-martial). The oath s used for court members will be those 
prescribed in the discussion to RCM 807(b)(2).

A.4. Reporters Any court reporter, military or civilian, may be given a one-time oath. The oath normally 
will be administered by trial counsel in the first court-martial to which the court reporter 
is assigned. Once such oath is taken, the court reporter need not be sworn again at any 
trial to which assigned. When the court reporter is not sworn at trial, the fact that he or 
she has been previously sworn will be stated on the record (i.e. captured on the digital 
audio file of the recording of the court-martial). The following oath may be used in 
administering a one-time oath to court reporters, see RCM 807(b)(2) discussion: 
Do you swear (or affirm) that you will faithfully perform the duties of reporter to this 
court-martial (, so help you God)?

______________________________

A.5. Interpreters Interpreters will be sworn by trial counsel as provided in the discussion to RCM 
807(b)(2).

A.6. Recording one-time The certified counsel who gave the oath prescribed above for reporters and interpreters 
oaths of reporters and will transmit a signed copy to the Office of Military Justice (CG-LMJ). When a court 
interpreters  reporter is a member of the Coast Guard assigned copy of the oath taken by the court 
reporter will be filed in the court reporter’s official service record. The copies must bear 
below the signature of the person sworn a statement signed by the person who 
administered the oath, in a form as follows: 
[Quote the Oath and state purpose why administered; followed by] 
The undersigned personally administered the foregoing oath to the above-named 
[Person Sworn], this ____ day of __________, 20____, at [Location]. 
Signature and Rank

Section B. Matters for Sessions Held Pursuant to Article 39(a), UCMJ

B.1. Arraignment and As authorized under Article 39(a), UCMJ, a session of the court without the presence of 
pleas the members may be called for the purpose of holding the arraignment and receiving the 
pleas of the accused. See CG Rules of Court.

B.2. Pretrial motions  Pretrial motions will be submitted in accordan ce with the orders of the military judge 
assigned to the case, and, unless otherwise permitted or ordered by the military judge 
assigned to the case, will follow the format prescribed in the CG Rules of Court.

B.3. Custody and Physical restraint must not be imposed on the accused during open sessions of the court-
restraint of accused  martial unless prescribed by the military judge. See RCM 804(e)(3).

B.4. Conditional pleas The accused may enter a conditional plea of guilty in accordance with RCM 910(a)(2). 
Such plea must be in writing and must set forth the particular pretrial motion(s) to which 
the right of appeal is reserved. Because conditional guilty pleas subject the government 
to substantial risks of appellate reversal and the expense of retrial, SJAs should consult 
with the Chief, Office of Military Justice (CG-LMJ) prior to the government’s consent 
regarding an accused entering a conditiona l guilty plea at court-martial. Once this 
coordination is complete, the government’s consent to the plea may be given by trial 
counsel and must be noted on the document over the signature of the trial counsel.

B.5. Remote Article The use of audiovisual and teleconferencing t echnology is authorized by, to the extent 
39(a) sessions and under the conditions allowed for in, RCM 804(b), 805(a), 805(c).

B.5.a. When remote Article 39 (a), UCMJ, sessions will be called by order of the military judge. Either 
Article 39(a), UCMJ counsel, however, may make a request to the military judge that such a session be called. 
sessions may be The military judge of a general or special court-martial may, at an Article 39(a), UCMJ, 
authorized session, arraign the accused, hear argument and rule on motions, and receive the pleas 
of the accused during a remote Article 39(a), UCMJ session. If the accused pleads guilty, 
the military judge may at that time make the appropriate inquiry into the providence of 
the accused's plea. The military judge may also at that time accept the plea of the 
accused. Upon acceptance of a plea of guilty, the military judge is authorized to enter a 
finding of guilty immediately, except when the plea is to a lesser included offense and 
the prosecution intends to proceed to trial on  a greater offense. In a case where the 
accused pleads guilty, the military judge may hear evidence on sentencing and sentence 
the accused.

source: Coast Guard Military Justice Manual (COMDTINST M5810.1H)

e military judge is authorized to enter a

B.5.b. Procedure for 
remote Article 39(a), 
UCMJ sessions

B.5.c. Requirements for 
remote Article 39(a), 
UCMJ sessions 
Consistent with RCM 804 and 805, the military judge may order the use of audiovisual 
technology, such as video teleconferencing technology, among the parties and the 
military judge for purposes of Article 39(a), UCMJ, sessions if all parties consent, or if 
the benefit of conducting the remote Article 39(a), UCMJ, session significantly 
outweighs its cost. Use of such audiovis ual technology will satisfy the “presence” 
requirement of the accused on ly when the accused has a defense counsel physically 
present at the accused’s location. Such technology may include two or more remote sites 
as long as all parties can see and hear each other. 
The audiovisual technology employed must be capable of providing real time, two way 
communications of video images and sound such that the accused and the military judge 
can see all participants, and the court reporter is able to record everything said in all 
locations. A transcript of remote Article 39(a), UCMJ, sessions will be produced 
verbatim, capturing a written record of all pro ceedings, transcribing everything said in 
all locations. Testimony of witnesses during remote Article 39(a), UCMJ, session must 
not take place in a location other than th e location of the accused unless the accused 
affirmatively waives the right to have the witness present in the same location on the 
record, or such witness testimony is otherwise permitted under the Military Rules of 
Evidence or Rules for Court-Martial outside of the presence of the accused.

Section C. Release of  Information at  Trial  
  
C.1 References   The information contained in  this Section is a synopsis of the information provided in  
the Court-Martial Public Affairs Guide available on the Office of Military Justice (CG-
LMJ) website ( https://uscg.uscg.afpims.mil/Resources/Legal/Military-Justice/) and 
should  be referred to for more complete information than is provided in this Section.

C.2. Generally   The task of striking a fair balance betw een the protection of members accused of  
offenses against improper  or unwarranted  publicity pertaining to their cases, and  public  
understanding of the problems of controllin g misconduct in the armed forces and the  
workings  of military justice depends largely on the exercise of sound judgment by those 
responsible for administering military justice and  by  representatives of the press and 
other news media. At the  heart of all  guidelines  pertaining to the  furnishing of  
information concerning an  accused, or the allegations against him or her, is the  
prohibition against providing statements or information to  news media for the purpose 
of, or which reasonably could have the effect of, influencing the outcome of a trial.

C.3. Applicability   These provisions apply to all persons who may obtain information as the result of duties 
performed in  connection with the  processing of an accused, the investigation of  
suspected  offenses, or trials by court-martial. These provisions are applicable from the 
time of apprehension, the referral of charges, or the commencement of an investigation 
directed to make  recommendations  concerning  disciplinary action, until the completion  
of trial or disposition  of the case without trial. These provisions also prescribe guidelines  
for the release or dissemination of information to public news agencies, other public  
media, or  other persons or agencies.

C.4. Responsibilities As a general matter, Coast Guard employees should  not  initiate release of information 
regarding the release of pertaining to  an accused. Information of this  nature should  be released  only  upon  
information  specific request, and, subject to the following guidelines, should  not exceed the scope  
of the inquiry concerned.

C.4.a. Proactive public Determination  of  whether a case should be the subject of a  proactive public affairs stance 
affairs stance   should  be developed in conjunction with the convening authority, the servicing legal 
office, and the cognizant public affairs officer. For sexual assault related cases,  
amplifying guidance should be obtained from the Office of the Judge Advocate General

(CG-094) and the Office of Governmental & Public Affairs (CG-092). A final decision 
on the release rests with the convening authority.

source: Coast Guard Military Justice Manual (COMDTINST M5810.1H)

(CG-094) and the Office of Governmental & Public Affairs (CG-092). A final decision

C.4.b. Procedure Except in unusual circumstances, in formation subject to release under these provisions 
should be reviewed and released by the cognizant public affairs officer, and requests for 
information received by others from the media should be referred to such officer for 
action. In high visibility cases a trained attorney should assist the cognizant public affairs 
officer. When a member is suspected or accused of an offense, care should be taken to 
indicate that the member is alleged to have committed an offense or is suspected or 
accused of having committed an offense, as distinguished from stating or implying that 
the accused has committed the offense(s).

C.5. Information subject When deemed appropriate, the following information concerning a member accused or 
to release suspected of an offense(s) may generally be released, except as provided in Subsection 
18.C.7:

a. The accused’s name, grade, age, unit, and regular assigned duties;

b. The substance of the offense(s) of which the individual is accused or suspected;

c. The identity of the apprehending and investigating agency and the identity of 
the attorney for the accused, if any;

d. The type and place of custody, if any;

e. Information that has become a part of the record of proceedings of the court-
martial in open session;

f. The scheduling or result of any stage in the judicial process; and

g. The denial of the commission of any offense(s) of which he or she may be 
accused or suspected (when release of such information is  approved by the 
attorney of the accused).

C.6. Information The following information normally is not released except as provided in Subsection 
prohibited from release  18.C.7:

a. Subjective opinions, observations or comments concerning the accused’s 
character, demeanor at any time, or guilt of the offense or offenses involved;

b. The prior criminal record (including other apprehensions, charges, or trials) 
or the character or reputation of the accused;

c. The existence or contents of any confession, admission, statement or alibi 
given by the accused, or the refusal or failure of the accused to make any 
statement;

d. The performance of any examination or test, such as polygraph examinations, 
chemical tests, ballistics tests, etc., or the refusal or the failure of the accused 
to submit to an examination or test;

e. The identity, testimony, or credibility of any crime victim;

f. The identity, testimony, or credibility of possible witnesses;

g. The possibility of a plea of guilty to any offense charged or to a lesser offense 
and any negotiation or any offer to negotiate respecting a plea of guilty;

h. References to confidential sources or investigative techniques or procedures; 
and

f. The identity, testimony, or credibility of possible witnesses;

g. The possibility of a plea of guilty to any offense charged or to a lesser offense

h. References to confidential sources or investigative techniques or procedures; 
and

i. Any other matter when there is reasonable likelihood that its dissemination

C.7. Exception cases The provisions of this Section are not intend ed to restrict the release of information 
designed to enlist public assistance in appr ehending an accused or suspect who is a 
fugitive from justice or to warn the public of any danger that a fugitive accused or 
suspect may present. Further, because the purpose of this Section is to prescribe 
generally applicable guidelines, there may be circumstances that warrant the release of 
information normally not released in accordance with 18.C.6 above, or the non-release

of information authorized for release under 18.C.5 above. In these cases, the servicing 
legal office will advise the convening authority on whether such material should be 
released.

C.8. Sealed materials  Materials ordered sealed in accordance w ith RCM 1113 may only be disclosed to 
specific individuals per the procedures of that rule.

C.9. Military judge’s 
authority 
The foregoing is in no way to be viewed as in derogation of the authority of the military 
judge, who, upon being detailed, is, in his or her discretion, empowered to regulate many 
of the matters discussed in this Section by court order. See RCM 801.

Section D. Spectators 
D.1. Preliminary 
hearings  
The sessions of a preliminary hearing conv ened in accordance with Article 32, UCMJ, 
should ordinarily be open to the public. See RCM 405(j)(3). A convening authority will 
consult with the servicing legal office before restricting access to a preliminary hearing. 
D.2. Courts-martial Sessions of courts-martial will be public and, in general, all persons except those who 
may be required to give evidence will be admitted as spectators. But see Article 6b(a)(3), 
UCMJ. The trial counsel must post a Notice of Public Trial. A template Notice of Public 
Trial is available on the Office of Military Justice (CG-LMJ). Spectators or classes of 
spectators may be excluded only when, in the discretion of the military judge of a general 
or special court-martial, he or she determines such action to be legally necessary and 
proper. See RCM 806. Summary courts-martial officers must consult with the servicing 
legal office before restricting access to a summary court-martial proceeding.

source: Coast Guard Military Justice Manual (COMDTINST M5810.1H)
section: Chapter 19 -- Government Appeals from Adverse Rulings
section_title: Interlocutory Appeals u nder Article 62, UCMJ
heading_path: Interlocutory Appeals u nder Article 62, UCMJ
text:
Government Appeal from Adverse Rulings at Trial and Petitions for Extraordinary Relief

Section A. Interlocutory Appeals u nder Article 62, UCMJ

A.1. Authority   Article 62, UCMJ, and Rule for Court Martial (RCM) 908  authorize the United  States 
to appeal a ruling or order of the military judge that terminates the proceedings with 
respect to a charge or specification, excludes evidence that is substantial proof of a fact  
material to the proceedings, jeopardizes the security of classified material, or a military  
judge’s entry of a finding  of not guilty following a finding  of guilty by the members.

A.2. Procedures for trial  If trial counsel wishes to  seek an appeal under Article 62, UCMJ, written notice of the 
counsel   intent to do so  must be received by the mi litary judge within 72 hours of the order or 
decision that gives rise to the appeal pursuant to RCM 908(b)(3). Written  notice must 
contain both the date and time that the notice was served on  the military judge. In any 
case in which trial counsel wishes to file an Article 62, UCMJ, appeal, trial counsel  
should if practicable confer with appellate government counsel at the Office of Military 
Justice (CG-LMJ) prior to  providing  written notice of its intent to appeal. Trial counsel  
may request a recess from the court in  order to  do so. Trial counsel must present a  
summary of the ruling  or order, the issue involved, and the evidence in the case and 
describe the reasons why trial counsel believes the ruling  or order is one that should  be  
appealed. After the consultation, appellate government counsel will advise trial counsel  
on  whether to file notice of intent to appeal. If trial counsel cannot confer with appellate 
government counsel in a reasonable time, trial counsel must file the written notice within 
72  hours to  preserve the right to appeal, and should contact  the Office of Military Justice 
(CG-LMJ) as soon as practicable thereafter. The 72-hour rule is an inflexible rule and 
must be strictly adhered to. If trial counsel wishes to seek reconsideration of a ruling, 
and contemplates that he  or she will seek an  Article 62, UCMJ, appeal if reconsideration  
is denied, the request for reconsideration must come within  72 hours of the order or 
decision. The appellate courts do  not have  jurisdiction to  hear an interlocutory appeal  
where trial counsel waited longer than 7 2 hours before seeking reconsideration and then  
sought to file an  Article 62, UCMJ, appeal  within 72 hours of  the reconsideration denial.

A.3. Procedure for Once advised  of the matter, appellate government counsel will authorize trial counsel to 
appellate government advise the military judge and  parties that:  
counsel   a.  The government will not appeal the ruling, in which case the trial should 
proceed; or

A.4. Forwarding notice Trial counsel must e-mail a notice of intent  to appeal filed under RCM 908 to appellate

LMJ@uscg.mil as soon as practicable after it is filed. Under RCM 908(b)(6), the trial 
counsel must also provide a summary of the proceedings and relevant facts, a statement 
of the error assigned and relief requested, notice of appeal, and the Record of Trial 
(ROT) in the form described in Section  19.B.

(ROT) in the form described in Section  19.B.

Section B. Record of Trial  
  
B.1 Forwarding to the The authenticated ROT must be received by the Offi ce of Military Justice (CG-LMJ)  
Office of Military within  20  days from the date the notice of appeal is filed to  comply with the Joint Court 
Justice (CG-LMJ)  of Criminal Appeals rules (See Court of Criminal Appeals Rule 22). The trial counsel  
must mail the ROT to the Office of Military Justice (CG-LMJ) by  DHS-approved 
commercial carrier. Only that portion  of the ROT involving the alleged error(s) need be  
transcribed, authenticated, and forwarded.

B.1.a. Retention of copy  Trial counsel should retain  a copy  of the authenticated ROT, so that trial may resume  
of ROT by trial counsel  after the Coast  Guard  of Criminal Appeals issues a decision, if authorized. 
  
B.2. Extraordinary cases  In extraordinary cases where au thentication  is expected to take more than 20  days, the 
Office of Military Justice (CG-LMJ) may re quest an enlargement of time from the 
CGCCA in accordance with the United States  Coast Guard Court of Criminal Appeals 
Rules of Practice and Procedure.  When  delay is expected, trial counsel must inform the 
Office of Military Justice (CG-LMJ) of the expected  delay, and the reasons for the delay, 
as early as possible. In cases where there is  delay, trial counsel must e-mail the matters  
required un der RCM 908(b)(6), and an unauthenticated ROT to the Office of Military  
Justice (CG-LMJ), HQS-DG-LST-CG-LMJ@uscg.mil, to be followed  by the 
authenticated record as soon as possible.  
  
B.3. Writing the appeal   The Office of Military Justice (CG-LMJ) is responsible for writing and filing the appeal  
and has final authority on  what issues are contained within  the brief. Appellate counsel 
will consult with trial counsel throughout the writing  process. Chief, Office of Military 
Justice (CG-LMJ), is the final authority on whether the appeal will be filed. If at any  
time after notice of intent to  file has been  given and before the filing of the appeal, the 
Chief, Office of Military Justice (CG-LMJ) determines that the appeal is not in the best  
interest of the Coast Guard, notice will be made to trial counsel in  writing  of that 
decision, and the trial will resume.  
  
B.4. Authority to  After notice of a decision by the Coast Guard Court of Criminal Appeals or Court of  
conduct further Appeals for the Armed Forces is provided, th e trial may proceed, if authorized, in a 
proceedings   manner consistent with the decision, unless further proceedings are stayed by order of a  
higher court. 
  
B.5. Retention of  If the trial proceeds, the Office of Military Justice (CG-LMJ) will forward all original  
Records  appellate motions, briefs, opinions, etc. to the trial counsel to include in the ROT, as an 
appellate exhibit.

Section C. Petitions for Extraordinary Relief  
  
C.1. References  The process for filing, and  responding to, petitions for extraordinary relief, including 
those under Article 6b(e), UCMJ, is outlined in the United  States Coast Guard Court of  
Criminal Appeals Rule of Practice and Procedure 19.

source: Coast Guard Military Justice Manual (COMDTINST M5810.1H)
text:
Sentencing Matters

Section A. Sentencing System 
A.1. Applicable 
sentencing system 
Only one system applies in a court-martial. If any charged offenses occurred before the 
implementation of the Military Justice Act of 2016 (January 1, 2019) the previous 
unitary sentencing system will apply unless the accused opts into the new system 
under RCM 902A(b). Under the Military Justice Act of 2016, the following UCMJ 
Articles are applicable to sentencing: 
 Articles 16(c)(2) and 19(b), UCMJ (a special court-martial consisting of 
judge alone); 
 Article 25(d), UCMJ (sentencing by members); 
 Article 39(a)(4), UCMJ (sentencing within a 39(a) session); 
 Article 52(b)(2), UCMJ (votes required for sentencing); 
 Article 53, UCMJ (findings and sentencing); 
 Article 56(a), UCMJ (sentence maximums); 
 Article 56(b), UCMJ (sentence minimums for certain offenses); 
 Article 56(c), UCMJ (imposition of sentence, including segmented sentencing 
by military judge versus unitary sentencing by members); 
 Article 58a, UCMJ (reduction in enlisted grade upon approval). 
B.1. No automatic 
reduction  
B.2. Hard labor without 
confinement

Section B. Court-Martial Punishment Limitations 
As a matter of policy, administrative action of automatic reduction to the lowest 
enlisted pay grade as provided under Article 58a, UCMJ, is not authorized in the Coast 
Guard. This policy does not preclude reduction to the lowest enlisted pay grade being 
adjudged and approved as part of a lawful sentence. 
Hard labor without confinement is performed in accordance with the same regulations 
applicable to extra duties imposed as NJP under Article 15, UCMJ. See Subsection 
2.M.5.  
C.1. Approved by 
Director, Reserve and 
Military Personnel (CG-
13) 
C.2. Not approved by 
Director, Reserve and 
Military Personnel (CG-
13)

Section C. Reservists Ordered to Active Duty 
A reservist tried by court-martial after an order approved by Director, Reserve and 
Military Personnel (CG-13) to active duty pursuant to Subsection 4.J.2 may receive 
any punishment otherwise authorized by the court-martial. 
Unless the order to active duty is approved by the Director, Reserve and Military 
Personnel (CG-13), confinement is not an authorized punishment for a reservist 
ordered to active duty at court-martial. In addition, the reservist accused may not be 
retained on active duty to serve any other punishment, see discussion to RCM 
1003(c)(3). Imposed forfeitures may be collected, and imposed restrictions on liberty 
may be served during subsequent periods of inactive duty training or active duty.

source: Coast Guard Military Justice Manual (COMDTINST M5810.1H)
text:
ive duty at court-martial. In addition, the reservist accused may not be 
retained on active duty to serve any other punishment, see discussion to RCM 
1003(c)(3). Imposed forfeitures may be collected, and imposed restrictions on liberty 
may be served during subsequent periods of inactive duty training or active duty.

Section D. Sentences Including Reprimands  
D.1. Distinguished from Reprimands issued in execution of court-martial sentences are punitive reprimands 
administrative non- (and to be distinguished from administrative non-punitive reprimands) and are 
punitive measures required to be in writing and detailed in the member’s PDR.

D.1.a. Issuance  Punitive letters of reprimand adjudged  by a court-martial are issued  by the convening  
authority and are contained  within the entry of judgment under RCM 1111(b)(3)(D). 
See 1003(b)(1).   
  
D.1.b. Content   The punitive letter of reprimand must include the time and place of trial, type of court, 
 and a statement of the charges and specifications of which convicted. It must also  
 contain the following paragraph:  
 A copy  of this letter will be placed in your  official record  at Coast Guard 
 Headquarters.  You may forward to Coast  Guard Personnel  Service Center (PSC-psd-
 mr) within 15  days after receipt of this action such statement concerning this letter as  
 you may desire for inclusion in your record. In connection with your statement, you 
 are advised that any statement submitted  must be couched in temperate language and 
 must be confined to pertinent facts. Opinions must not  be expressed nor the motives of  
 other personnel impugned. Your statement must  not contain countercharges. 
  
D.1.c.  SJA responsibility  If a punitive letter of reprimand is adjudged, the SJA must ensure completion and  
submit the letter to the convening authority for approval at the of time action.  
 
  
D.1.d.  Delivery to the The original letter must be delivered to  the accused personally or by First  Class 
accused  Certified Mail or a DHS-approved commercial carrier that provides a delivery receipt 
 or tracking number. If personally delivered, the accused must acknowledge receipt of 
 the punitive letter of reprimand  on Personnel Service Center’s copy  of the letter to be  
 filed in the member’s official record.  
  
D.1.e. Procedure  A copy  of the letter will be appended to the convening authority action. The action  
should refer to  the letter in the following tenor: 
Pursuant to the sentence of the court a letter of reprimand  is this date being served  
upon the accused and a copy thereof is hereby incorporated as an integral  part of this  
action.  
  
D.1.d. Forwarding copy to  Forwarding Copy to Coast  Guard Personnel  Service Center. A copy  of the letter of  
Coast Guard Personnel reprimand, with either the accused’s acknowledgment of receipt or the signed return 
Service Center receipt attached, will be forwarded to Coast Guard Personnel Service Center (CG PSC-
bops-mr). 
  
D.1.e. Appeals   Review, including appellate review, of letters of reprimand issued as part  of an  
approved court-martial sentence will be accomplished as provided for by the UCMJ, 
the Manual for Courts-Martial (MCM), and this Manual with  respect to the 
proceedings of the particular  court-martial that imposed the sentence. No  separate  
appeal from these letters will be considered.

source: Coast Guard Military Justice Manual (COMDTINST M5810.1H)

imand issued as part  of an  
approved court-martial sentence will be accomplished as provided for by the UCMJ, 
the Manual for Courts-Martial (MCM), and this Manual with  respect to the 
proceedings of the particular  court-martial that imposed the sentence. No  separate  
appeal from these letters will be considered.

Section E. Effective Date of Court-Martial Sentences

E.1. Generally  Unless deferred a sentence of confinement begins to  run on the date the sentence is  
adjudged. See RCM 1102(b)(2). Any adjudged  reduction in pay grade and/or adjudged  
or automatic forfeiture of pay and allowances will take effect in accordance with 
Articles 57(a)(1) and  58b, UCMJ. Those approved portions of the sentence that do  not 
extend to death, dismissal, or  dishonorable or bad conduct discharge will normally  be  
ordered executed in the initial convening authority action, unless suspended  or  
deferred in that action.

E.2. De ath or dismissal   Sentences to death may be approved only by the President. A sentence of dismissal 
may be approved only  by the Secretary  of the Department of Homeland Security, or 
the Secretary of the Navy if the Coast Guard is operating as  a service under the Navy. 
Only the Secretary can order executed an  approved death sentence or dismissal.

E.7.  Statutory 
forfeitures pursuant to 
Article 58b, UCMJ 
Unless deferred, any period of confinement included in a court-martial sentence begins 
on the date the sentence is adjudged, regardless of whether the accused is already 
confined.  See Art 57(b), UCMJ. 
If requested by the accused, a sentence to confinement may be deferred by the 
Convening authority or by the officer exercising general court-martial jurisdiction 
(OEGCMJ) over the accused if the accused is no longer in the Convening authority’s 
command.  This request must originate with the accused and must be in writing. 
Deferment will end in accordance with RCM 1103(f).  Any request for deferment of 
confinement and the written action on the request must be included in the Record of 
Trial (ROT). 
No dishonorable or bad conduct discharge may be ordered executed until the 
conviction is final under RCM 1209.  The OEGCMJ may order the sentence executed 
in all cases where the accused has waived appellate review and review under RCM 
1201 is final.  The OEGCMJ must inform the Coast Guard Personnel Service Center 
thru the Office of Military Justice (CG-LMJ).  See Subsection 20.E.4.c. 
In all other cases, even after the conviction is final, the punitive discharge may not be 
ordered executed until the ROT has been reviewed for clemency in accordance with 
Ch.1.F.6.d(4), Discipline and Conduct, COMDTINST M1600.2 (series), and the 
discharge approved by Commandant.  When clemency review is completed the Office 
of Military Justice (CG-LMJ) will advise whether clemency was granted or denied 
pursuant to Article 74(a), UCMJ, and notify the Coast Guard Personnel Service Center 
of the type of discharge to be issued. 
If a punitive discharge is approved in the initial action, the discharge is self-executing 
in accordance with RCM 1102(b)(3) when the Office of Military Justice (CG-LMJ) 
notifies the Coast Guard Personnel Service Center of the type of discharge to be 
issued. 
Article 57(a), UCMJ, makes reductions in pay grade effective 14 days after being 
adjudged.  Upon application of the accused the convening authority or the OEGCMJ 
over the accused may defer the effective date of reduction in pay grade at any time 
until entry of judgment or in the case of a summary court-martial, when the convening 
authority acts on the sentence.  Deferment may be rescinded based on information not 
previously considered.  The Coast Guard by policy does not authorize the 
administrative reduction in pay grade authorized under Article 58a, UCMJ.  See 
Subsection 20.B.1. 
Article 57(a), UCMJ, makes adjudged forfeitures of pay and allowances effective 14 
days after being adjudged.  Upon application of the accused, the convening authority or 
the OEGCMJ over the accused may defer the effective date of forfeitures at any time 
until entry of judgment or in the case of a summary court-martial, when the convening 
authority acts on the sentence.

source: Coast Guard Military Justice Manual (COMDTINST M5810.1H)

days after being adjudged.  Upon application of the accused, the convening authority or 
the OEGCMJ over the accused may defer the effective date of forfeitures at any time 
until entry of judgment or in the case of a summary court-martial, when the convening 
authority acts on the sentence.

Deferment may be rescinded based on information not 
previously considered. 
Article 58b, UCMJ, provides for administrative forfeiture of pay and allowances 
during confinement for a member sentenced by a court-martial to a period of 
confinement exceeding 6 months or any period of confinement accompanied by a 
punitive discharge.  Forfeitures under Article 58b, UCMJ, are not an adjudged court-
martial sentence.

E.8.b. An order waving 
administrative forfeitures

E.8.c.  Procedure 
If Article 58b, UCMJ, administrative forfeitures apply in the case of a general court-
martial, all pay and allowances are automatically forfeited during the period of 
confinement.  In the case of a special court-martial, two-thirds of pay is automatically 
forfeited during the period of confinement.  The amount forfeited by operation of 
Article 58b, UCMJ, is that amount above any adjudged and approved fines or 
forfeitures.  The total adjudged fines and forfeitures and administrative forfeitures may 
not exceed the jurisdictional limit of the court-martial.  Article 58b, UCMJ, 
administrative forfeitures automatically take effect, but may be deferred. 
In cases where a member subject to Article 58b, UCMJ, administrative forfeitures has 
dependents, all or part of the forfeiture may be waived by the convening authority any 
time after sentencing for a period not to exceed 6 months, provided that the amounts 
waived are paid directly to the accused’s dependents. 
Administrative forfeiture under Article 58b, UCMJ, is a congressionally required 
administrative (not a courts-martial sentencing or punitive) action applicable only if a 
prisoner is otherwise entitled to pay.  There are circumstances in which a member is 
convicted and sentenced to confinement and is not entitled to, or loses entitlement to 
military pay.  Notable is the loss of entitlement to pay when a prisoner’s enlistment 
expires while in confinement (e.g., a prior extension does not become effective if the 
member is confined and a prisoner is precluded from reenlisting) or if a member’s 
enlistment has expired but he or she is involuntarily extended for purposes of courts-
martial (the member is automatically released from active duty and assumes a non-pay 
status upon announcement of the sentence). 
An order deferring any forfeitures or reduction in pay grade should be signed by the 
convening authority and worded substantially as follows: 
The (forfeitures/reduction in pay grade) adjudged on (date sentence adjudged) in the 
case of (accused’s name, EMPLID, and unit) by the (Special/General) Court-Martial 
convened by my order number (convening order number) dated (date) are hereby 
deferred from (effective date of deferral) until (end date of deferral/date action is taken 
on the sentence). 
An order waiving administrative forfeitures should be signed by the convening 
authority or person acting under Article 60, UCMJ, and RCM 1103 and worded 
substantially as follows: 
Any forfeitures arising by operation of Article 58b, UCMJ, as a result of the sentence 
adjudged (date sentence imposed) in the case of (accused’s name, EMPLID, and unit) 
by my (Special/General) Court-Martial Order (CM Order Number) dated (date) are 
hereby waived as follows: Effective date of waiver, (date); End date of waiver,(date --
not to exceed 6 months from start date); Amount of forfeitures waived per month 
(stated in whole dollar amounts).

source: Coast Guard Military Justice Manual (COMDTINST M5810.1H)

(stated in whole dollar amounts).

This waiver applies only to forfeitures arising by 
operation of Article 58b, UCMJ, and does not apply to adjudged forfeitures.  All 
amounts waived are to be paid to the accused’s dependents. (If applicable -- This 
waiver is granted at the request of (the accused, defense counsel, the accused’s 
spouse, etc.). (Attach copy of any request.)) 
Deferral and waiver orders may be combined in a single document.  This document 
may be the convening authority’s action; however, deferral decisions may be made 
prior to the action.  A certified copy of any order waiving administrative forfeitures, or 
deferring forfeitures or reductions in pay grade, must be provided immediately to the

Servicing Personnel Office responsible for the accused’s service record. Orders 
denying deferral requests should include the reasons for denial. See RCM 1103(d)(2) 
All original orders must be attached to the ROT.

E.9. Effective date of 
other punishment 
All other punishments adjudged by a court-martial take effect on the date ordered 
executed. See Article 57, UCMJ.

E.9. Effective date of 
other punishment 
All other punishments adjudged by a court-martial take effect on the date ordered

Section F. Payment of Fines and Restitution 
F.1. Payment of fines The accused must pay fines by mailing or delivering a check or money order payable 
to the U.S. Treasury to the Pay and Personnel Center (PPC), Topeka, Kansas: 
Commanding Officer (SES) 
USCG Pay and Personnel Center 
444 SE Quincy Street 
Topeka, KS 66683-3591 
F.2. Payment of 
restitution 
The Coast Guard utilizes a lockbox facility to receive payment for restitution as part of 
a pretrial or plea agreement (i.e. the repayment of misused or stolen funds by an 
accused). To make restitution, the accused must mail a check or money order payable 
to “The United States of America” to: 
USCG Accounts Receivable 
PO Box 979119 
St. Louis, MO 63197-9001 
Express Mail (Street Address for Courier) Lockbox No. 979119 U.S. Bank 
Government Lockbox 
ATTN: GOVERNMENT LOCKBOX, SL-MOC1 GL 
1005 Convention Plaza 
St. Louis, MO 63101-1229 
A copy of associated paperwork (promulgating order and when appropriate of the plea 
agreement) should be sent with the check so that the receiver will know what it is for 
and a copy should go to the trial counsel as proof of payment.

Section G. Appellate Rights Advisement 
G.1. Appellate rights 
notification 
Each convicted accused must be advised of his or her appellate rights, prior to 
adjourning the court-martial. A completed appellate rights notification must be 
included as an appellate exhibit in the record of trial. See RCM 1010. A template 
Appellate Rights Notice is available on the Office of Military Justice (CG-LMJ) 
website, https://uscg.uscg.afpims.mil/Resources/Legal/Military-Justice/.

source: Coast Guard Military Justice Manual (COMDTINST M5810.1H)
section: Chapter 21 -- Post-Trial Processing in General and Special Courts-Martial
chapter_title: Post-Trial Processing in General and Special Courts-Martial
heading_path: Post-Trial Processing in General and Special Courts-Martial
text:
Post-Trial Processing in General and Special Courts-Martial, Cases Referred After January 1, 2019

Section A. Statement of Trial Results (ST R )  
 
A.1. Generally    After final adjournment of a  general  or special court-martial, the military judge must  
sign a STR in accordance with Rule for Courts-Martial (RCM) 1101. The military judge 
may direct the trial counsel to  prepare a draft STR for the military judge’s signature. The 
Statement of Trial Results available on the Office of Military Justice (CG-LMJ) website, 
https://uscg.uscg.afpims.mil/Resources/Legal/Military-Justice/  may be used. The 
original STR  will be maintained with the record  of trial (ROT).  
  
A.2. National Instant In addition to the requirements of RCM 1101, the STR, or an attachment to the STR, 
Criminal Background  must affirmatively state if the conviction re quires or does not require entry of the 
Check System (NICS) accused into the National Instant Cr iminal Background Check System  in accordance 
with  18 U.S.C. §  922. Entry is required  if the trial results in  a conviction:   
a.  At a general  court-martial for any offense punishable by a sentence to 
confinement exceeding one year, regardless of the sentence imposed; 
b.  In which the accused receives a dishono rable discharge or, for officers, a  
dismissal; 
c.  For an  offense  of domestic violence, Article 128b, UCMJ; and  
d.  For a violation of Article 112a, UCMJ.  
The Office of Military Justice (CG-LMJ) will review all STR’s to ensure compliance 
with this subsection.  
  
A.3. DNA collection  In addition to the requirements of RCM 1101, the STR, or an attachment to the STR, 
must affirmatively state if the conviction requires DNA  collection. In  accordance with  
10 U.S.C. § 1565, DNA samples are required for each person who is convicted of any 
offense under  the UCMJ punish able by a sentence to conf inement for more than  one  
year, regardless of the punishment imposed.

Section B. Trial Counsel Responsibilities after Trial

B.1.  Distribution   When trial counsel receives the STR, trial co unsel must promptly e-mail, or otherwise 
provide, a copy to the following personnel:

a.  The accused’s commanding officer or immediate commander;

b.  The Convening  Authority (CA);

c.  The Office of Military Justice (CG-LMJ);

d.  Defense counsel;

e.  The servicing personnel office for the accused;

f.  The Office of Member Advocacy and Legal Assistance (CG-LMA);

g.  The servicing CGIS office, regardless of  whether that office was involved in  
investigating the case;

h.  Any crime victim or victim’s counsel in the case, without regard to  whether the 
accused was convicted or acquitted of any offense;

i.  The military judge; and

j.  The officer in  charge  of the confinement facility (if applicable).

B.2. Confinement order  If the accused  is sentenced to confinement, trial counsel is responsible for preparing  a 
Confinement Order, using the DD Form 2707, and making arrangements for the accused 
to be taken immediately to the confinement facility after adjournment.

B.3. Notice to crime Immediately after final adjournment, trial counsel must provide crime victims, through 
victims of  right to  counsel, if applicable, a notice of the victims’ right to submit matters under RCM  
submit matters  under  1106A, and the procedures for doing so.  A template Victim  Notification is available on  
RCM 1106A the Office of Military Justice (CG-LMJ) website,

https://uscg.uscg.afpims.mil/Resources/Legal/Military-Justice/. For the purpose of this 
notice, “crime victim” means an individual who has suffered direct physical, emotional, 
or pecuniary harm as a result of the commission of an offense of which the accused was 
found guilty, and on which the CA may take action under RCM 1109 or 1110, or that 
person’s representative.

B.4. RCM 1106 and Upon request from the accused, any crime victim, and/or their counsel pursuant to RCM 
1106A matters 1106 and 1106A, trial counsel shall provide: (1) a copy of the recordings of all open 
sessions of the court-martial; and (2) copies of or access to the evidence admitted at the 
court-martial, and appellate exhibits. Such access shall not in clude sealed or classified 
material or recordings unless authorized by the military judge upon a showing of good 
cause. Per the United States Coast Guard – Courts-Martial System of Records, the 
recordings, evidence, and appellate exhibits may be released to these parties or their 
counsel without redactions. The court reporter, under supervision of trial counsel, must 
prepare electronic copies for access by removing all sealed or classified matters and 
closed portions of the trial, unless such access has been authorized by the military judge. 
The recordings of all open se ssions of the court-martial will be sent to the accused or 
crime victim (or their counse l) electronically. Copies of  or access to the evidence 
admitted at the court-martial, and appellate exhibits will be coordinated by trial counsel. 
See RCMs 1106(c) and 1106A(d).

Section C. Exercise of Convening Authority’s (CA’s) Powers

C.1. Applicability If the accused is found guilty of a specification alleging the commission of one or more 
offenses before January 1, 2019, Article 60 UCMJ, as in effect on the date of the earliest 
offense of which the accused was found guilty shall apply to the CA, to the extent that 
the applicable Article 60, UCMJ:

a. Requires action by the CA on the sentence;

b. Permits action by the CA on findings;

c. Authorizes the CA to modify the findings and sentence of a court-martial;

d. Authorizes the CA to dismiss any char ge or specification by setting aside a 
finding of guilty thereto;

e. Authorizes the CA to change a finding of guilty to a charge or specification to 
a finding of guilty to an offense that is a lesser included offense;

f. Authorizes the CA to order a proceeding in revision or rehearing; or

g. Authorizes the convening authority to approve, disapprove, commute, or 
suspend a sentence in whole or in part. 
Additionally, Article 60a(c), UCMJ as enac ted by the Military Justice Act of 2016, 
which permits the suspension of certain sentences upon recommendation of the military 
judge, applies in all cases regardless of the date of the offense(s).

C.2. Legal advice The requirement that the SJA or servicing legal officer make a recommendation to the 
required CA has been eliminated from the RCM. However, prior to taking action as described in 
Subsection 21.C.1, the CA must consult with the CA’s SJA or servicing legal officer. 
The SJA or servicing legal officer should document the consultation in a memo to file, 
which is not included in the ROT.

C.3. When it is 
impracticable for CA to 
exercise powers

C.3.a. Special courts- When it is impracticable for the special court-martial convening authority (SPCMCA) 
martial to act as described in Subsection 21.C.1, the case will be forwarded to the officer 
exercising general court-martial jurisdiction (OEGCMJ) with a statement of the reason 
why the SPCMCA did not act. The OEGCMJ may take action or, in that officer’s 
discretion, refer the case to any other subordinate SPCMCA for action.

C.3.b. General  courts- When it is impracticable for the general court-martial convening authority (GCMCA) to  
martial act as described in  21.C.1, the GCMCA’s servicing legal  office must submit a request 
to the Office of Military Justice (CG-LMJ) to designate another GCMCA to take action.  
Once the Office of Military Justice (CG-LMJ) has designated an alternate GCMCA to 
act, the servicing legal office will send the case to the designated officer, along with a 
statement of the reason why the original GCMCA did not act.

C.3.c. Matters forwarded  When a case is forwarded to  another CA, the original CA’s servicing legal office will

C.3.d. Matters added to  The statement explaining why the original CA did not act, along with  the Office of

C.4. Form  The CA Action and Entry of Judgment form available on the Office of Military Justice  
(CG-LMJ) website, https://uscg.uscg.afpims.mil/Resources/Legal/Military-Justice/  
may be used.

C.5. Notification of  Immediately after the CA takes action or decides to take no  action, the SJA or servicing  
military judge  legal officer will notify the military judge and provide any required matters as described  
in RCM 1109(g) or  1110(e).

Section D. Entry of Judgment  
  
  
 
 
    
  
  
 
  
  
 
  
 
  
  
   
 
  
  
   
 
  
 
 
  
   
 
 
 
  
D.1
. Generally Within five working days of receipt of the notification in 21.C.4, or, in cases of acquittal, 
within five working days of final adjournm ent of the court-martial, the military judge 
must enter judgment as provided in RCM 1111. The military judge may direct trial 
counsel or the servicing legal office to prepare a draft entry of judgment.  
D.2. Form The CA Action and Entry of Judgment form available on the Office of Military Justice 
(CG-LMJ) website, https://uscg.uscg.afpims.mil/Resources/Legal/Military-Justice/ 
may be used. The original CA Action and Entry of Judgment form will be maintained 
with the record of trial (ROT).  
D.3. Distribution  Upon receipt of the Entry of Judgment from the military judge, trial counsel will e-mail 
a copy to the following: 
a. The accused’s commanding officer; 
b. The CA; 
c. The Office of Military Justice (CG-LMJ) (as part of the ROT); 
d. Defense counsel; 
e. The servicing CGIS office; 
f. CG-LMA; 
g. Any crime victim or victim’s counsel in the case; 
h. The Servicing Personnel Office maintaining the service record of the accused 
for appropriate service record entries (o r, in the case of a retired member, the 
Pay and Personnel Center); 
i. PSC (adm-3) 
j. The place of confinement (if the accused is still in confinement); and 
k. The military judge. 
D.4. Publication  The commander of the accused or the convening authority may publish the Entry of 
Judgment to their respective commands.

text:
ce/. The court reporter, in 
coordination with the trial co unsel, is ultimately responsible for the accuracy and 
completeness of the ROT. Once the ROT has been certified for completeness and 
accuracy, the court reporter will then send electronically the certified ROT with certified 
transcript to the military judge.

E.4.a. Verification by the Upon receipt, from the court reporter, the mi litary judge will verify that the ROT is a 
military judge complete record of the proceedings. Any missing items will be noted and if necessary 
the ROT will be returned to the court reporter for correction. Once the completeness of 
the ROT has been verified, the military judge will note his or her verification to the ROT 
and return it to the court reporter.

E.5. Verbatim transcript A verbatim transcript of the audio recording contained in the ROT per RCM 1112(b)(1) 
of the audio recording is required in any case where the judgment entered into th e record includes a sentence 
of death, dismissal, dishonorable or bad-conduct discharge, or confinement for more

than six months. See RCM 1114(a)(1). In such cases, the servicing legal office is 
responsible for sending a copy of the audio recording to a contracted transcriptionist as 
soon as practicable after final adjournment, to be completed in no more than 30 days 
from receipt. Alternatively, the servic ing legal office may pr epare an in-house 
transcription, as long as that transcription is completed no more than 30 days after final 
adjournment. The person who prepared the verbatim transcript should attach a document 
attesting that it is a true, accurate, and complete transcript of the audio recording of the 
court-martial proceeding. If a separate digital recording exists for sealed materials, a 
separate transcript shall be prepared and se aled. A filler page referencing the sealed or 
closed portions of the court-martial proceedings will be inserted in the appropriate place 
in each transcript.

E.6. Attachments for Unless the accused was found not guilty of all charges and specifications, the following 
appellate review  must be attached to each general or special court-martial certified ROT before it is sent 
to the Office of Military Justice (CG-LMJ) for review under Article 65 or 66, UCMJ:

a. Any transcription of the court-martial proceedings created pursuant to RCM 
1114 and Subsection 21.E.5;

b. The preliminary hearing report under Article 32, UCMJ, if any;

c. Waiver of preliminary hearing under Article 32, UCMJ, if any;

d. The pretrial advice under Article 34, UCMJ, if any;

e. The record of any former hearings, if the trial was a rehearing or new or other 
trial of the case;

f. Written special findings, if any, of the military judge;

g. Exhibits, or, with the permission of the military judge, copies, photographs, or 
descriptions of any exhibits which were marked for and referred to on the 
record, but not received in evidence;

h. Any matter filed by the accused or vi ctim under RCM 1106 or 1106A, or any 
written waiver of the right to submit such matters;

i. Any deferment request and the action on it;

e, copies, photographs, or 
descriptions of any exhibits which were marked for and referred to on the 
record, but not received in evidence;

h. Any matter filed by the accused or vi ctim under RCM 1106 or 1106A, or any 
written waiver of the right to submit such matters;

i. Any deferment request and the action on it;

j. Conditions of suspension, if any, and proof of service on probationer under

k. Any waiver or withdrawal of appellate review under RCM 1115;

l. Records of any proceeding in connection with a vacation of suspension of the 
sentence under RCM 1108;

m. Modification of judgment;

n. Any redacted materials; and

o. Any post-trial motions and record of proceedings (prepared and certified).

E.7. Format Checklists for arrang ement and formatting of the ROT, verbatim transcript, and 
attachments for appellate review can be fo und on the Office of Military Justice (CG-
LMJ) website, https://uscg.uscg.afpims.mil/Resources/Legal/Military-Justice/.

E.7.a. Recording format All co urt-martial proceedings, except those that are sealed or closed, must be included 
in an audio file. All sealed or closed portions should be included in a separate audio file, 
and must be password protected. In each case other than one in which the accused was 
acquitted of all charges and specifications, the court reporter or transcriptionist must 
create an index showing time-stamps indica ting where each session of court or trial 
testimony begins in the audio recording.

E.7.b. Digital portions of Any portion of the ROT, such as a video or audio recording of an interview, should be 
the record indicated in a filler page within the ROT.

E.7.c. Marking Mark all exhibits with an exhibit number, and in the case of multiple exhibit pages, the 
page number. Prosecution exhibits are labeled with Arabic numerals, defense exhibits 
are labeled with capital letters, and appellate exhibits are labeled with Roman numerals.

Draw a line through parts of the marking that do not apply. Sealed exhibits must be 
marked prior to sealing.

E.7.d. Sealed exhibits Except as noted in Subsection 21.E.7.e for exhibits containing child pornography, 
exhibits that have been sealed by the military judge must be included in the ROT. The 
pages of a sealed exhibit must be marked in the same way as other exhibits prior to being 
sealed. The sealed items must be removed by  the court reporter or by the Office of 
Military Justice (CG-LMJ) and examination and disclosure must be in accordance with 
RCM 1113.

source: Coast Guard Military Justice Manual (COMDTINST M5810.1H)

text:
military judge must be included in the ROT. The 
pages of a sealed exhibit must be marked in the same way as other exhibits prior to being 
sealed. The sealed items must be removed by  the court reporter or by the Office of 
Military Justice (CG-LMJ) and examination and disclosure must be in accordance with

E.7.e. Child pornography In order to ensure compliance with the Adam  Walsh Act, 18 U.S.C. § 3509(m), sealed 
sealed exhibits exhibits containing child pornography must not be copied, and the actual child 
pornography material must not be included with the original ROT. At the conclusion of 
trial, the original sealed exhibit containing child pornography must be turned over to the 
servicing Coast Guard Investigative Service office. A one-page memorandum 
explaining these procedures and where the sealed exhibit is located will be inserted in 
place of the exhibit. Once the ROT is complete and received by the Office of Military 
Justice (CG-LMJ) the original sealed exhibit containing child pornography will be sent 
from the servicing CGIS office to CGIS Headquarters, in accordance with CGIS policy 
and requirements for transportation of such contraband, where it will be held pending 
appellate review. When a case is final in accordance with RCM 1209, the Office of 
Military Justice (CG-LMJ) will inform CGIS Headquarters and the child pornography 
exhibits may then be destroyed.

E.8. Additional post-trial For more detailed instructions on how to conduct post-trial processing, please review 
instructions the LMJ Post-Trial Processing Guide on th e LMJ Portal Page. Please use the included 
checklists to ensure a timely completion of post-trial processing.

Section F. Disposition of ROT 
Each special or general court-martial certifie d ROT must be sent electronically by the 
court reporter to the Office of Military Justice (CG-LMJ) as soon as possible upon 
receipt of military judge’s verification. Unless the accused was found not guilty of all 
charges and specifications, the attachments in Subsection 21.E.6 must be included.  The 
hard-copy original ROT and attachments in Subsection 21.E.6. (if applicable) must be 
sent to the Office of Military Justice (CG-LMJ) by a DHS-approved commercial carrier 
(e.g., FedEx or UPS).  Digital copies must be retained by the office preparing the ROT, 
either on a server, removable media, or both. 
The Office of Military Justice (CG-LMJ) records custodian shall forward the ROT with 
the attachments in Subsection 21.E.6 to the CG CCA and appellate defense counsel for 
all cases eligible for automatic appellate re view and when the accused exercises his or 
her right to direct appeal. See RCM 1116. 
At the same time the ROT and attachments for appellate review (if applicable) are 
forwarded to the Office of Military Justice (CG-LMJ), the court reporter must send the 
ROT (see Subsection 21.E.3.) to the accused, either personally or through counsel, 
according to the preferences an d information provided by th e accused in the post-trial 
election of rights form. Per the United States Coast Guard – Courts Martial System of 
Records, the ROT may be released to the accused or their counsel without redactions. 
Sealed exhibits may only be distributed in accordance with RCM 1113.  
Per the United States Coast Guard – Courts Martial System of Records, the ROT may 
be released to a victim or their counsel without redactions. However, to protect the 
privacy interests of any person other than the victim, personally identifiable information 
within the ROT must be redacted. See RCM 1112(e)(4). A redacted transcript of the

substantially verbatim recording of the c ourt-martial may be provided in place of a  
redacted  recording of the court-martial. Sealed exhibits  may only  be distributed in  
accordance with RCM 1113.

offense of which the  reporter must notify any victim who testifie d (regardless of the findings as to the  
accused was charged in specification(s) concerning that victim), of the opportunity  to receive the ROT.   If the  
the victim testified  during  victim requests the ROT, the Office of Military Justice (CG-LMJ) must provide a copy  
the proceedings  per Subsection 21.F.3.

F.3.b. Victims named in a If a named  victim requests the ROT, the Office of Military Justice (CG-LMJ) must 
specification  of which the provide a copy, per Subsection 21.F.3.

Section G.  Additional  National Instant Background  Check System (NICS) Requirements  
G.1.
 Generally In accordance with Coast Guard Investig ative Service Roles and Responsibilities, 
COMDTINST 5520.5 (series), CGIS enters, and,  as appropriate, requests removal of, 
information in NICS, the National Crime Information Center (NCIC) database, and the 
Interstate Identification Index (III or “triple-eye”). 
G.2. Upon referral to Trial counsel will forward a copy of the ch arge sheet in any case where a convening 
general court-martial authority refers charges for trial by a general court-martial to the servicing CGIS office 
that handled the investigation or would provide investigative services to the convening 
authority for the case for entry in the National Instant Background Check System 
(NICS). The copy must be sent as soon as practicable after referral. 
G.3. If accused is found If an accused is found not competent to st and trial under Article 76B, UCMJ, or not 
not competent to stand guilty due to lack of mental responsibility under Article 50a UCMJ, trial counsel shall 
trial  forward a copy of the finding to the servicing CGIS office and inform the servicing 
CGIS office that the finding requires entry in NICS.

G.4. STR Each STR will note if entry into NICS is required and will be forwarded to CGIS. See 
Subsection 21.A.2.

Section H. Coast Guard Policy on National Sex Offender Registry Notification and Registration 
Requirements

H.1. Generally If a member of the Coast Guard has been convicted of a sexually violent offense or 
certain offenses against a minor, the Coast Guard is required to provide notice to federal, 
state, and local officials prior to the member's release from confinement, and the member 
may be required to register as a sex offender under state law.

H.2. Notification upon 
Conviction 
State sex offender registry and United States Marshals Service National Sex Offender 
Targeting Center notifications for service members convicted as sex offenders under the 
UCMJ who are incarcerated in a DoD Compon ent confinement facility will be carried 
out in accordance with procedures in DoDI 1325.07, Administration of Military 
Correctional Facilities and Clemency and Parole Authority. Confinement facilities are 
notified that a member is subject to this notification requirement by a required annotation 
on the Report of Results of Trial Memorandu m or Statement of Trial Results, which is 
distributed to the confinement facility upon completion of trial.

H.3. Statement of Trial 
Results and Entry of 
Judgment 
If a Coast Guard member is convicted of an offense referred to trial on or after 1 January 
2019, the appropriate information must be included in the Statement of Trial Results and 
the Entry of Judgment. 
H.4 Qualifying Offenses DoDI 1325.07 includes a list of offenses  that trigger sex offender notification 
requirements. Note: Sex offender registration requirements vary by state and may be 
triggered by offenses not listed in DoDI 1325.07. A member convicted of an offense that 
does not trigger sex offender notification requirements under DoDI 1325.07 may still be 
required to register as a sex offender under state law.

H.5. Questions 
Regarding Registration 
When a question arises whether a conviction triggers notification requirements, SJAs 
should seek guidance from CG-LMJ. 
H.6 Confinement 
Facility Responsibilities 
The Confinement Officer, or designee respons ible for custody of the inmate, ensures 
compliance with federal and state laws with  regards to sex offender registration and 
notifications to civilian agencies, in accordance with the requirements detailed in DoDI 
5525.20, Registered Sex Offender (RSO)Management in Department of Defense, which 
is applicable to the Coast Guard. For those Coast Guard members convicted at a court-
martial of an offense listed in Enclosure 2 of 1325.07, Administration of Military 
Correctional Facilities and Clemency and Parole Authority who are confined, the 
confinement facility is responsible for ensuring the DD Forms 2707-1 and 2791 are filled 
out and filed appropriately.

H.7. No Post-trial 
Confinement 
When compliance with this direction is required, but confinement is not part of the 
adjudged punishment (or sufficient pretrial or illegal pretrial confinement credit 
completely offsets the term of confinement imposed at trial):

H.7. No Post-trial 
Confinement 
When compliance with this direction is required, but confinement is not part of the 
adjudged punishment (or sufficient pretrial or illegal pretrial confinement credit 
completely offsets the term of confinement imposed at trial):

a. SJA Responsibilities. The SJA shall notify the Special Agent in Charge (SAC) 
of the Coast Guard Investigative Service Region in writing, within twenty-four 
hours of the member's conviction. This notification occurs via distribution of 
the Statement of Trial Results for charges and specifications referred to court-
martial on or after 1 January 2019, Repor t of Results of Trial for charges and 
specifications referred to court-martial prior to 1 January 2019. It also occurs 
via filling out the DD Form 2707-1 and DD Form 2791.

b. CGIS Responsibilities. The SAC will ensure that the DD Form 2707-1 and 
2791 are distributed as required, and appropriate criminal database entries

made, for those cases where the member is convicted on an offense listed in 
enclosure (2) of 1325.07, Administration of Military Correctional Facilities 
and Clemency and Parole Authority , but not sentenced to confinement or 
pretrial confinement credit offsets the period of confinement to which the 
member was sentenced.

text:
Review of Courts-Martial, Cases Referred After January 1, 2019

Section A. Review of Certain Special and General Cour ts-Martial Not Eligible for Direct or Automatic 
Appeal

A.1. Background  For Special and General Courts-martial where there is a finding  of  guilt and the sentence 
does not include confinement for six months  or more or a punitive discharge, initial 
review of the record is performed under Article 65(d), UCMJ.  After initial review  under 
Article 65(d), UCMJ, an accused may petition TJAG to modify or set aside the findings  
or sentence under Article 69(a), UCMJ.

A.2. Article 65(d),   
UCMJ review of cases 
not eligible for direct 
appeal

A.2.a. Review  by Judge The staff judge advocate (SJA) of the of ficer exercising general court-martial 
Advocate jurisdiction (OEGCMJ) will assign a judge advocate on  his or  her staff to  complete the  
mandatory review of the certified ROT of all courts-martial under this Section. If no 
judge advocate is available per Rule for Court-Martial (RCM) 1201(c)(2), the SJA  will 
request that the Office of Military Justice (CG-LMJ) review the ROT. See Article 
65(d)(2), UCMJ and RCM  1201(a)-(d).

A.2.b. Procedure  Upon receiving th e certified record of trial for a co urt-martial, the reviewing judge 
advocate assigned will conduct a review per RCM 1201(d). A template RCM 1201  
review memorandum can be found  on the Office of Military Justice (CG-LMJ) website, 
https://uscg.uscg.afpims.mil/Resources/Legal/Military-Justice/.

A.2.c. Corrective action If the reviewing  judge advocate assigned believes no corrective action is required, the  
not required  review memorandum stating a written conclusion as to each matter set out in RCM 
1201(d) must be attached to the record of tr ial. The review is then considered final and 
the ROT must be sent to the Office of Military Justice (CG-LMJ) by  DHS-approved 
commercial carrier (e.g., FedEx or UPS) for retention.

A.2.d. Corrective action If the reviewing judge advocate assigned believes corrective action may be required, the 
required   certified ROT must be sent to The Judge Ad vocate General (TJAG) thru the Office of 
Military Justice (CG-LMJ) by DHS-approved commercial carrier (e.g., FedEx  or  UPS) 
with the review memorandum stating a wri tten conclusion as to each matter set out in 
RCM 1201(d). TJAG may set aside the findings and sentence in  whole or in part in  
accordance with Article 65(e), UCMJ and  RCM 1201(f). If TJAG takes action, the 
action  will be documented  on a separate memorandum, and returned  with the record to 
the Office of  Military Justice (CG-LMJ) for retention. Conversely, if TJAG declines to 
take the recommended corrective action, the review is complete, and the record is 
returned to the Office of Military Justice (CG-LMJ) for retention. The review  is 
considered final upon return of the record  to the Office of Military Justice (CG-LMJ).

A.2.f. Personnel ineligible Per RCM 1201(c)(2), any personnel who  has acted in the same case as an accuser, 
to conduct Article 65(d), preliminary hearing officer, member of the court-martial, military judge, or counsel, or  
UCMJ review  has otherwise  acted on  behalf of the prosecution or defense.

A.3. Article 69, UCMJ 
review

A.3.a. Accused submits 
an application to TJAG

A.3.b. Timing of 
application

A.3.c. Submission by a 
person other than the 
accused

A.3.d. Action by TJAG

A.3.e. Processing by the 
Office of Military Justice 
(CG-LMJ) after action by 
TJAG

A.3.f. Referral to the CG 
CCA following TJAG 
action

A.3.g. Accused submits 
an application to CG CCA 
to review TJAG’s action 
When Article 65(d), UCMJ review is fina l, the accused may submit an application 
requesting TJAG to modify or set aside, in whole or in part, the findings and sentence. 
See Article 69(a), UCMJ.  A member of Commandant (CG-094C) shall review the 
application as an attorney designated by TJAG, and make a recommendation as to what 
action, if any, should be taken. 
The accused must submit an application to TJAG via the Office of Military Justice (CG-
LMJ), not later than one year after receiving a copy of the results of the Article 65(d), 
UCMJ review and TJAG’s action on that review, if any, by the Office of Military Justice 
(CG-LMJ). See Article 69(b), UCMJ and RCM 1201(h)(2). TJAG may, for good cause 
shown, extend the period for submission of an application for a time period not to exceed 
two additional years. See Article 69(b), UCMJ and RCM 1201(h)(3). 
If the application is submitted by a person ot her than the accused, that applicant must 
show authority to act on behalf of the accused. See RCM 1201(h)(5).  
TJAG may set aside the findings or sentence, in whole or in part in accordance with 
Article 69(c), UCMJ and RCM 1201(h)(4)(A). TJAG’s action, or decision not to take 
any action, will be documented in a separate memorandum.  The ROT and memorandum 
will be returned to the Office of Military Justice (CG-LMJ). 
The Office of Military Justice (CG-LMJ) must  send TJAG’s action, or notification of 
TJAG’s decision to not take action,  to:

i. The accused; 
ii. Trial counsel; 
iii. Personnel Service Center SJA or the accused’s SPO (if necessary to correct 
service record and Direct Access court-martial report); 
iv. PPC (if the sentence was affected by the action);

MJ) must  send TJAG’s action, or notification of 
TJAG’s decision to not take action,  to:

i. The accused; 
ii. Trial counsel; 
iii. Personnel Service Center SJA or the accused’s SPO (if necessary to correct 
service record and Direct Access court-martial report); 
iv. PPC (if the sentence was affected by the action);

v. Trial defense counsel; 
vi. Crime victim within the meaning of Article 6b, UCMJ and Special Victims 
Counsel (if applicable); 
vii. Counsel filing the petition if different from Trial defense counsel; 
viii. SJA to the Convening Authority; and 
ix. Military Judge. 
The original copy of the action will be kept with the ROT. The notification will be made 
pursuant to the procedures outlined in Subsection 22.E.1. See Subsection 22.A.3.f. for 
processing when TJAG refers his or her action to the Coast Guard Court of Criminal 
Appeals (CG CCA) for review.  
TJAG may forward his or her action to the CG CCA for review. If  TJAG does so, the 
action, the record of trial, and a referral memorandum will be provided to the CG CCA 
by the Office of Military Justice (CG-LMJ) . The accused will be notified of TJAG’s 
action, the referral, and his or her right to have appellate defense counsel by the Office 
of Military Justice (CG-LMJ); see the notification procedures outlined in Subsection 
22.E.1. See also RCM 1201(k)(1)(A). 
The accused may also appeal the action of TJAG via application to the CG CCA. See 
Article 66(b)(1)(D), UCMJ, Article 69(d)(1)(B), UCMJ and RCM 1201(k). The 
application must be submitted not later than 60 days after the date the accused is notified 
of TJAG’s decision. The accused does not have the right to appellate defense counsel to 
assist with the submission. See Article 69(d)(3), UCMJ. Petitions for review are 
submitted directly to the CG CCA in acco rdance with court rules. If a petition is

submitted, the CG CCA will notify the Offi ce of Military Justice (CG-LMJ), and CG-
LMJ will provide the ROT to  the CG CCA.

A.3.h. Notification of C G  The Office of Military Justice (CG-LMJ) must notify the accused  of any  decision  of the 
CCA’s decision or  CG CCA (e.g., rejecting the petition,  or accepting the petition and issuing an op inion) 
opinion  as per Subsection  22.E.1., and retain a copy of the CG CCA decision or  opinion  with  
the ROT.

Section B. Direct Appeal of Certain Special and General Courts-Martial 
B.1. Background Pursuant to Article 66(b)(1)(A-D), UCMJ the accused may directly appeal cases to CG 
CCA when: 
a. The sentence includes confinement for mo re than six months and the case is 
not subject to automatic review; 
b. The Government previously filed an appeal in the case under Article 62, 
UCMJ;  
c. TJAG sent the case to the CG CCA for review of the sentence under Article 
56(d), UCMJ; or 
d. An application for review was submitted under Article 69(d)(1)(B), UCMJ was 
granted by CG CCA.  See Section 22.A.3.g. 
For sentences including confinement for two years or more or a punitive discharge see 
Section 22.C. 
B.1.a. Notice of right to In any courts-martial under this Section, where the accused has not waived or  withdrawn 
appeal his or her right to an appeal under Article 61, UCMJ, the Office of Military Justice (CG-
LMJ) must, as soon as practicable after receipt of the certified RO T, provide notice to 
the accused or counsel of the right to appeal in accordance with Subsection 22.E.1. See 
Article 65(c), UCMJ and RCM 1116(b)(2). CG-LMA will be provided a copy of the 
notification. The notification will be attached to the ROT. 
B.1.b. Forwarding of If the accused has not waived or withdrawn his or her right to appeal under Article 61, 
ROT UCMJ and appellate defense counsel has been detailed, CG-LMA must inform the 
Office of Military Justice (CG-LMJ). Per Ar ticle 65(b)(2)(A)(i), UCMJ, the Office of 
Military Justice (CG-LMJ) must promptly forward a copy of the ROT and attachments 
for appellate review to appellate defense counsel and the CG CCA. 
B.1.c. Timeline for filing For an appeal to be timely, it mu st be filed in accordance with the timeline detailed in 
Article 66(c), UCMJ. 
B.1.d. Disposition of After the CG CCA completes its review and is sues an opinion, the ROT, together with 
cases after CG CCA the opinion, will be returned to the Office of Military Justice (CG-LMJ).  The Office of 
completes review Military Justice (CG-LMJ) will provide the accu sed with a copy of the opinion. If the 
accused has the right to petition CAAF, the accuse d must also be notified of that right, 
and the applicable timelines for petitioning CAAF, in writing.  See RCM 1203(f)(2). 
Procedures for providing CG CCA opinions to the accused are described in Subsection 
22.E.1.  
B.1.e. Review when If the accused fails to submit a timely appeal, his or her case will be reviewed by a judge 
Appellate does not appeal advocate at The Office of Military Justice (CG-LMJ). See Article 65(d)(3), UCMJ and 
RCM 1201(e).
