Section C. Automatic Review of Certain Special and General Courts-Martial 
C.1. Background 
C.2. Forwarding cases 
for automatic review  
C.3. Withdrawal from 
appellate review  
C.4. Processing upon 
decision by the CG CCA 
Unless the accused waives appellate review under RCM 1115, each case in which the 
judgment includes a sentence of dismissal of a commissioned officer, cadet, or 
midshipman, dishonorable discharge or bad-conduct discharge, or confinement for 2 
years or more is reviewed by the CGCCA under Article 66(b)(3), UCMJ. 
Upon receipt of a ROT and attachments fo r appellate review, the Office of Military 
Justice (CG-LMJ) will forward the ROT and attachments to the CG CCA and detailed 
appellate defense counsel.  
Withdrawal from appellate review may be filed with the Office of Military Justice (CG-
LMJ). If the accused withdraws his or her case from appellate review under RCM 1115, 
the Office of Military Justice (CG-LMJ) will send a copy of the withdrawal to the CG 
CCA. Upon return of the ROT, the Office of Military Justice (CG-LMJ) will attach the 
withdrawal to the ROT, and review the case under Subsection 22.E.1. 
After the CG CCA completes its review and is sues an opinion, the ROT, together with 
the opinion, will be returned to the Office of Military Justice (CG-LMJ).  The Office of 
Military Justice (CG-LMJ) will provide the accu sed with a copy of the opinion. If the 
accused has the right to petition the CAAF, the Office of Military Justice (CG-LMJ) 
must also notify the accused of that right, and the applicable timelines for petitioning 
CAAF.  See RCM 1203(f)(2). Procedures for serving CG CCA opinions on the accused 
are described in Subsection 22.E.1.

source: Coast Guard Military Justice Manual (COMDTINST M5810.1H)

opy of the opinion. If the 
accused has the right to petition the CAAF, the Office of Military Justice (CG-LMJ) 
must also notify the accused of that right, and the applicable timelines for petitioning 
CAAF.  See RCM 1203(f)(2). Procedures for serving CG CCA opinions on the accused 
are described in Subsection 22.E.1.

Section D. Review When the Accused Waives, Withdraws, or Has Not Filed an Article 66(b), UCMJ Appeal

D.1. Review under When the accused waives his or her Article 66(b), UCMJ, appeal, an attorney within the 
Article 65(d)(3), UCMJ Office of the SJA to the Convening Authority shall review the case under Article 
65(d)(3), UCMJ and RCM 1201(e). When the accused withdraws his or her Article 
66(b), UCMJ, appeal, or when  the accused fails to file a timely appeal under Article 
66(b), UCMJ, a member of CG-094C shall review the application as an attorney 
designated by TJAG under Article 65(d)(3), UCMJ and RCM 1201(e). If after review, 
the reviewing attorney believes correctiv e action may be required, the reviewing 
attorney must prepare a memorandum for TJAG documenting the review and 
recommending an appropriate corrective action.

D.1.a. Processing An acc used shall submit his notice of withdrawal from appellate review to the Office of 
Military Justice (CG-LMJ). CG-LMJ shall review the notice of withdrawal for 
compliance with RCM 1115(d).  After review, CG-LMJ shall transmit the notice of 
withdrawal to the Chief Judge of the CGCCA.  The Chief Judge, or another Judge the 
Chief Judge designates, will then review the withdrawal for compliance with RCM 
1115, and conduct the Article 65 review. 
If corrective action is recommended and TJAG concurs that such action is required, he 
or she can set aside the findings and sentence in whole or in part.  See Article 65(e), 
UCMJ. TJAG must provide notice and a copy of the results of the review and any action 
he or she has taken to the accused per RCM 1201(g) and the notification procedures 
outlined in Subsection 22.E.1. CG-LMJ must retain the review with the ROT.

D.2. Petitions under After review under Article 65(d)(3), UCMJ is complete, the accused may submit an 
Article 69(c)(2), UCMJ application to TJAG in accordance with Article 69(b), UCMJ and RCM 1201(h). 
TJAG’s review under Article 69, UCMJ is limited to the issue of whether the accused’s 
waiver, withdrawal, or failure to file was invalid under the law. See RCM 1201(h)(4)(B).

D.2.a. Timing of  The accused must submit an application to TJAG via the Office of Military Justice (CG-
application   LMJ), not later than  one year after receiving  a copy of the results of the Article 65, 
UCMJ review and TJAG’s action  on that review, if any, by the Office of Military Justice 
(CG-LMJ). See Article 69(b), UCMJ and RCM 1201(h)(2). TJAG may, for good  cause 
shown, extend  the period for submission  of an application for a time period not to exceed 
two additional years. See Article 69(b), UCMJ and RCM 1201(h)(3).

Section E. Service of Notices  and CG CCA Decisions  
E.1.
 Service of notices

E.2. Service of CG CCA 
decisions on crime 
victim and Special 
Victims’ Counsel (SVC) 
E.3. Service of decisions 
following government 
Where the accused is serving on active duty , the Office of Military Justice (CG-LMJ) 
must e-mail a copy of the notification or opinions to the SJA for the OEGCMJ over the 
accused. The SJA must forward the matters to the accused’s immediate commanding 
officer, who must serve the accused, procur e the accused’s signature on the provided 
receipt as evidence of service, and return the executed form to the Office of Military 
Justice (CG-LMJ) through the chain of command, including the OEGCMJ. 
Where accused has been placed on appellate  leave, released from active duty, or 
discharged, the Office of Military Justice (CG-LMJ) must send notification of the 
opinion directly to the accused by First Class Certified Mail, return receipt requested. 
If the attempted service of the decision is unsuccessful, a certificate of attempted service 
will be prepared by the officer attempting service. The certificate will detail all attempts 
at service, and will be returned, along with the undelivered decision, to the Office of 
Military Justice (CG-LMJ) through the chain of command, including the OEGCMJ. 
Upon request of a crime victim as defined in Article 6b, UCMJ, or of SVC on the 
victim’s behalf, the Office of Military Justice (CG-LMJ) will provide a copy of the CG 
CCA decision to the crime victim. 
Following a decision of the CG CCA or the CAAF on a government appeal or a petition 
for extraordinary relief, the Office of Military Justice (CG-LMJ) will provide a copy of 
the decision to the trial counsel, defense counsel, CA, and military judge. Upon receipt

appeal or petition for of a copy of the decision, trial may proceed, if authorized, consistent with the decision 
extraordinary relief of the appellate court unless the trial is stayed by order of a higher court.

Section F.  Retention of Appellate Records   
  
F.1 Records produced  Upon completion of the CG CCA’s review and transmittal of the ROT back to CG LMJ, 
for/at the CG CCA the Office of  Military Justice (CG-LMJ) records custodian  will ensure that all original 
appellate petitions, motions, supplements, briefs, orders, and opinions are included  with 
the ROT.   
  
F.2. Records produced The Office of Military Justice (CG-LMJ) records custodian  will ensure that copies of 
for/at CAAF appellate petitions, supplements, motions, briefs, orders, and  opinions are included  with  
the ROT.

Section G. Petition for a New Trial under Article 73, UCMJ 
G.1.
 Time limitations At any time within three years after the date  of entry of judgment under Article 60c, 
UCMJ, an accused may petition TJAG for a new trial on grounds of newly discovered 
evidence or fraud on the court. 
G.2. Submission The accused must file the petition with TJAG  through the Office of Military Justice 
procedures (CG-LMJ). If the accused is still on active duty, he or she may file the petition through 
the chain of command. Filing the petition with any military authority tolls the time 
limitation. 
G.3. Content of petition The form and contents of petitions for new trial are specified in RCM 1210(c). Crime 
Victims within the meaning of Article 6b, UCMJ, must be provided notice of an 
application for a new trial. 
G.4. Action on the If the case is pending before the CG CCA or the CAAF, the petition will be referred for 
petition in cases pending action to that court. If referred for action to the Coast Guard Court of Criminal Appeals, 
appeal  that Court takes action in accordance with Courts of Criminal Appeals Rules of Practice 
and Procedure. 
G.5. Action on the In cases not pending appellate review, TJAG must take action in accordance with RCM 
petition in cases not 1210.   
pending appeal

Section H. Residual Clemency under Article 74(a), UCMJ 
H.1. Authority Residual clemency authority is derived from Article 74(a), UCMJ. Except for adjudged 
dismissals, the Secretary, Department of Homeland Security, delegated residual 
clemency authority to the Commandant, U.S. Coast Guard. See Department of 
Homeland Security Delegation Number 0170.1, available on the Office of Military 
Justice (CG-LMJ) website, https://uscg.uscg.afpims.mil/Resources/Legal/Military-
Justice/. The Commandant has the authority to remit or suspend any part or amount of 
the unexecuted part of any sentence, except those unexecuted sentences that must be 
approved by the President or the Secretary, Department of Homeland Security. 
H.2. Requirement for 
completion of appeal 
Residual clemency may not be granted while  a case is being reviewed by the Coast 
Guard Criminal Court of Appeals, U.S. Cour t of Appeals for the Armed Force, or the 
U.S. Supreme Court. 
H.3. Notice of decisions 
regarding residual 
clemency 
Crime Victims within the meaning of Article 6b, UCMJ, are entitled to notice of residual 
clemency decisions under Article 74(a), UCMJ, that offer any form of relief.

H.4. Procedure  For guidance on the residual clemency im plementation procedures, see Coast Guard 
Clemency Board, COMDTINST 5814.1 (series) and paragraph 1.F.6.d(4), Discipline 
and Conduct, COMDTINST M1600.2 (series).

Article 140a, UCMJ Implementation

Section A. Uniform Standards and Criteria Required by Article 140a

A.1. Purpose  This section outlines uniform standards and criteria for the military justice system 
pursuant to Article 140a, UCMJ, 10 U.S.C. § 940a.  Pursuant to Article 140a, the 
Secretary of Defense, in consultation wi th the Secretary of Homeland Security, 
prescribed uniform standards and criteria fo r the military justice system. The Judge 
Advocate General of the Coast Guard adopted these standards and criteria in full. 
A.2 Requirements and The data points and uniform definitions for collection by the services listed in the Table 
Responsibilities of Section A.3 shall be utilized when entering data into the Military Justice Case 
Management System. These data points shall be entered for all cases preferred on or 
after December 23, 2020. 
The Judge Advocate General of the Coast Guard has designated the Military Justice 
Case Management System as the official so urce for capturing, maintaining, tracking, 
and managing Coast Guard military justice data. 
The Servicing Legal Office responsible for a given case is also responsible for entering 
the data points required by Article 140a.  It is the responsibility of the trial counsel, 
supervised by the supported Staff Judge Advo cate to ensure that the data is accurately 
entered on a monthly basis. 
In appellate cases, the Senior Appellate Counsel, Office of Military Justice (CG-LMJ) 
is responsible for entering the data points required by Article 140a.

Section B. Public Access to Court-martial  Dockets, Filings, and Records Pursuant to  Article 140a 
B
.1. Purpose Article 140a, UCMJ  and the Secretary of Defense’s Uniform Standards for Article 140a 
require that the public have access to milit ary justice docket information, filings, and 
records to the greatest extent practicable.  All court-martial documents (dockets, filings, 
and court records) shall be redacted to comply with the Privacy Act, 5 U.S.C. § 552a, 
prior to being made accessible to the public. Docket information will be provided and 
updated on an ongoing basis.  Court filin gs and records will be made available in 
accordance with the instructions below. 
The processes and procedures directed by this chapter will ensure release of court-
martial information is conducted in accordance with appropriate statutory and regulatory 
provisions.  Section 23.C provides a complete list of information that must be redacted 
from all documents prior to posting on a public website.  A list of court filings or records 
that must be made available to the public is contained in Section 23.D . 
Records of trial, docket information, filings, and other records made publicly accessible 
shall restrict access to the Personally Identifiable Information (PII) of minors and 
victims of crime, as practicable to the extent such information is restricted in electronic 
filing systems of Federal and State Courts.  Federal courts require the following to be 
redacted from all filings: (1) social security numbers; (2) dates of birth; (3) names of 
minor children; (4) financial account numbers; and (5) home addresses. 
The release of court-martial records is also  governed by the Privacy Act.  The Coast 
Guard Freedom of Information and Priv acy Acts Manual, COMDTINST M5260.3 
(series), provides implementing guidance that  further restricts the release of certain 
additional categories of PII.  
The Coast Guard shall use redaction guidelines applicable under the Freedom of 
Information Act, 5 U.S.C. § 552 (FOIA). The FOIA requires the Coast Guard to disclose

information unless there is an exemption. The Coast Guard will apply FOIA redaction 
guidelines to prevent the release of certain sensitive information and protect personal 
privacy.

source: Coast Guard Military Justice Manual (COMDTINST M5810.1H)

Section C. Items to be Redacted

C.1. Items to be The following  shall be redacted  from all filings or court-martial documents to be made  
Redacted   publicly accessible:

a.  Social Security Numbers

b.  Tax Identification Numbers.

c.  Home Addresses.

d.  Telephone Numbers.

e.  Email Addresses.

f.  Date of Birth.

g.  Financial Account Numbers.

h.  Names of minors—completely redact or  utilize court-approved pseudonyms.

i.  Crime victim names—completely redact, or utilize court-approved  
pseudonyms.

j.  Coast Guard Employee ID number and DoD ID number.

k.  The name of any unit and any other identifying information referencing  
attachment to a unit overseas, a deployable unit (ship), or a special operations  
unit.

l.  Evidence or uncharged misconduct deem ed irrelevant or inadmissible by a 
military judge  under M.R.E 404(b).

m.  Evidence related to a victim’s or  witness’ ancillary misconduct deemed 
irrelevant  or inadmissible by a military judge.

n.  “Wet” (manual) signatures of all personnel.

o.  Law-enforcement names (5  U.S.C. § 552(b)(7)(C)).  If a Coast Guard  Trial 
Judge or the Coast Guard Court of Criminal Appeals (CGCCA) determines  
publishing the names of law enforcement personnel is in the interest of justice, 
such information must be  provided to the Office of Military Justice (CG-LMJ), 
and to  Military Justice Information Division (Code 41).

p.  All third-party  names, including witnesses or other persons named in any  filing  
or record (replace with initials, court-approved pseudonyms, or completely 
redact).  Also  redact the unit name and any other identifying  information, if the 
person is attached to a unit  overseas, a deployable unit (ship or  deployable  
specialized forces), or special forces .  (5 U.S.C. § 552 (b)(3),  (b)(6), and  
(b)(7)).  Leave the rank  of the military members unredacted.  If the Coast Guard 
Trial Judge and CGCCA determines publishing the names of witnesses (other  
than  victims or minors) is in the interest of justice, such information must be  
provided to the Office of Military Justice (CG-LMJ).

C.2. Items That Will Not 
Be Made Available

q. All panel members’ names, including excused members and members who sat 
on the court-martial, and alternate members (5 U.S.C. § 552(b)(6) and (b)(7)). 
Leave the rank of the military members unredacted.

r. All court-martial support personnel name s, including the court reporter and 
bailiff. (5 U.S.C. § 552(b)(7)(C)).  Leave the rank of the military members 
unredacted.  Do NOT redact the name of the counsel or military judge.

s. Information that could identify a co nfidential source or undercover law-
enforcement officer or operation. (5 U.S.C. § 552(b)(7)(D)).

t. Information that would reveal non-obvious law-enforcement techniques and 
procedures or guidelines for law-enforcement investigations or prosecutions. 
(5 U.S.C. § 552(b)(7)(E))

u. Information considered privileged in civil litigation.

Section D. Court-Martial Documents to be Made Publically Accessible 
D.1 Documents to be Pursuant to Article 140a, UCMJ the documents and records listed in this paragraph shall 
Made Publically be made accessible to the public after redac tion in accordance with Section 23.c.  No 
Accessible other documents will be released under Article 140a, UCMJ. 
Current dockets shall be accessible by the public for all the Coast Guard Trial Judiciary

and the Coast Guard Court of Criminal Appeals (CGCCA).  Schedules of CGCCA oral 
arguments and associated audio may be posted at the discretion of CGCCA.  
All motions and responses filed with the trial court, including bench briefs . The 
motion, response, or bench brief does not include the supporting evidence offered by a 
party in support of the motion or response.  All supporting evidence shall be filed as 
separate appellate exhibits, which shall not be released under Article 140a, UCMJ.  
Formal notices provided to the trial court.  Formal notices do not include emails sent 
to the trial court or notices submitted between counsel.  Formal notices to the trial court 
that will be made available to the public if submitted as a formal notice rather than an 
informal email include, but are not limited to, the following: 
1. Notice of appearance of civilian defense counsel or special victims’ counsel 
2. Government’s notice of appeal (R.C.M. 908) 
3. Parties’ notice of plea agreement (R.C.M. 910) 
4. Notice of pleas 
Requests submitted by counsel to the tr ial court as a formal, written filing . This 
does not include informal requests submitted to the trial court to address logistics (e.g. 
requests for RCM 802 conferences) or informal email requests.  Requests submitted by 
counsel to the trial court include, but are not limited to: 
1. Requests for relief from subpoena or other process (R.C.M. 309) 
2. Requests for forum composition (R.C.M. 503, 903) 
3. Requests for deposition (R.C.M. 702) 
4. Requests for R.C.M. 706 exam and the short-form report provided to the court, if 
applicable.  (Apply 5 U.S.C. § 552(b)(6) when determining information to redact 
from these documents).   
5. Requests for closed court-martial proceedings (R.C.M. 806) 
6. Requests to issue or amend protective orders (R.C.M. 701; M.R.E. 505, 506) 
7. Requests for instructions (R.C.M. 920, 1005) 
8. Requests for supplemental information from members, including voir dire (R.C.M. 
912) 
9. Requests for special findings (but not the special findings, see below) (R.C.M. 918). 
The following court records shall be made accessible to the public: 
1. Entry of Judgment 
2. Convening Authority’s Action including a punitive letter of reprimand, if applicable 
3. Statement of Trial Results 
4. Convening order(s)

5. Charge sheet 
6. Court rulings and orders.

Section E. Redaction of Dockets, Filings, and Court Records at the Trial Judiciary 
E.1 Dockets, Filings, and The following applies to the dockets, filings, and court records in the Coast Guard Trial 
Court Records at the Judiciary: 
Trial Judiciary 
1. Trial counsel, defense counsel, and special victims’ counsel shall minimize the use 
of PII and information exempt from release, as provided in this manual, to the 
maximum extent possible in all filings an d court records. Omit enclosures or 
attachments in all filings.  Any document ary evidence offered by the parties for 
consideration on a filing, will be submitted as a separate appellate exhibit, as 
directed by the military judge, and referenced as such in the filing, notice, request, 
or motion.  All supporting evidence filed as a separate appellate exhibit shall not be 
released under Article 140a, UCMJ. 
2. Upon certification of the re cord of trial, personnel responsible for post-trial 
processing shall transmit all certified records of trial to the Office of Military Justice 
(CG-LMJ). 
3. All trial court dockets must include th e following informa tion concerning each 
docketed case: (a) name of the case; (b) lo cation of the hearing; (c) forum (e.g., 
GCM or SPCM); (d) type of hearing (e.g., arraignment, Article 39(a), or trial); (e) 
the charges referred to th e court-martial; and (f) when appropriate, the military 
judge presiding over the hearing and the lead trial counsel and defense counsel 
assigned to the case. While the name of th e case, the location of the hearing, and 
the type of case will be updated on an ongoing basis, the military judge presiding 
over the hearing and the counsel assigned to the case may be updated and made 
accessible to the public at a reasonable time following the hearing. No docket will 
include any pre-referral hearings, such as Article 30a or Article 32 hearings. 
4. The Military Justice Information Division (Code 41), by agreement with CGJAG, 
will:

a. Not publish any court filings or records in cases that result in a full acquittal 
(not guilty to all charges and specifications.)

b. Review each digital record of trial and compile all filings and court records that 
will be made available to the public.

c. Redact all filings and court records to ensure each document is redacted in 
accordance with Section 23.c.

d.  If a military judge concludes evidence of  prior criminal activity is inadmissible 
at trial, unless otherwise ordered by the military judge, redact information that  
discusses the inadmissible evidence.

e.  Make the approved filings and court records publicly accessible.   The files will  
be uploaded onto the public facing website.

of Court-Martial to the outcome of the proceeding concerned, for not fewer than 15 years.

Military Justice Practitioners

Section A. Article 6, UCMJ Visits  
 
A
.1. Generally    Article 6,  UCMJ, requires the Judge Advocate General to  “make frequent inspection in  
the field in supervision  of the administration of military justice.” During these trips, the 
Judge Advocate General will endeavor to meet, to the extent  possible, with convening 
authorities, particularly those empowered to convene a general court-martial.

Section B. Certification of Counsel under Article 27(b), UCMJ 
  
B.1. Generally  In addition to the requirements stated in RCM 502, each active duty judge advocate is 
expected to meet the qualifications listed in  this Section in  order to become certified in 
accordance with Article 27(b), UCMJ. 
  
B.2. Designation as Each attorney serving in a legal program billet is expected to  obtain  designation as a 
judge advocate   judge advocate. Authority to designate attorneys as judge advocates has been delegated 
to the Judge  Advocate General. Requests for designation as a judge advocate may be  
made to TJAG in accordance with Chapter 11.C, Military Qualifications and Insignia, 
COMDTINST M1200.1. Requests are submitted in writing.  When requesting 
certification, in addition to the name of the bar in which admitted to practice law, a 
certificate of good standing from the member’s state bar must be transmitted to the 
Office of Legal Policy and Program Development (CG-LPD) to  complete the  
application.  
  
B.3. Basic Lawyer Each active duty judge advocate must successf ully complete the Basic Lawyer Course 
Course  conducted by the Naval Justice School or the Basic Judge Advocate Course conducted 
by the U.S. Army or Air Force. Waivers of  this requirement may be granted on a case-
by-case basis and must include a positive recommendation fro m the SJA requesting the 
waiver. Waiver requests should also includ e a listing  of all military justice on-the-job  
training such  as assistant counsel at courts-martial, comparable prior trial experience in  
civilian courts or previous certification in another military service. 
  
B.4. Certification  Personnel already designated as judge advocate will normal ly receive Article 27(b), 
UCMJ, certification and be sworn in upo n graduation f rom the Basic Lawyer Course at  
the Naval Justice School  (or the U.S. Army  or Air Force equivalent). Those attorneys  
who are unable to obtain designation as a judge advocate prior to the completion  of the 
Basic Lawyer Course must seek designation as a judge advocate and certification under 
Article 27(b), UCMJ, as soon as the re quirements in Chapter 11.C, Military  
Qualifications  and Insignia, COMDTINST M1200.1 (series) are met.

Section C. Professional Misconduct  
  
C.1. Generally   Subject to the limitations of Article 37, UCMJ, information of alleged personal  or  
professional misconduct by Coast Guard attorneys, including  willful and  wanton 
disregard for crime victim rights under Article 6b, UCMJ, should  be reported, together  
with appropriate supporting  information,  to the Judge Advocate General. For the  
purpose  of this  Section, “misconduct” is  defined as any act  or omission that is a violation  
of an applicable standard  of  professional responsibility or serves to  demonstrate the 
unfitness of the respective Coast Guard attorn ey to perform his or her legal duties. For 
the purpose of this Section,  “Coast Guard attorney” is defined as a military trial or 
appellate judge or an attorney practicing in proceedings governed  by the UCMJ and  
Manual for Courts-Martial (MCM). This Secti on does not affect any other criminal or 
administrative proceedings arising from  the underlying alleged misconduct. This

Section addresses only the authority of Coas t Guard military trial and appellate judges 
and attorneys to practice as a judge or attorney for the Coast Guard. 
C.2. Complaints  Complaints may be made in accordance with the Coast Guard Legal Responsibility 
Program Procedures for Reporting and Resolving Allegations of Professional 
Misconduct, Enclosure (3) to Guard Legal Professional Responsibility Program, 
COMDTINST M5800.1 (series). 
D.1. Professional 
responsibility

Section D. Standards of Conduct 
The Coast Guard Legal Professional Responsibility Program, COMDTINST M5800.1 
(series), applies to all Coast Guard attorneys. As far as practicable and not inconsistent 
with law, the MCM, and United States Coast Guard Regulations 1992, COMDTINST 
M5000.3 (series), the following American Bar Association Standards for the 
Administration of Criminal Justice are also applicable to Coast Guard courts-martial: 
the Prosecution Function and the Defense Function, the Function of the Trial Judge, and 
Fair Trial and Free Press. The Ameri can Bar Association Standards for the 
Administration of Criminal Justice are published and periodically updated by the 
American Bar Association.

text:
Article 138, UCMJ - Complaints

Section A. Definitions 
A.1. Generally An Article 138, UCMJ, complaint is a formal complaint against a Commanding 
Officer. Members of the Armed Forces who believe themselves wronged by their 
commanding officers, and have been refused redress by their commanding officers, 
may complain to a superior commanding officer, who then forwards the complaint to 
the officer exercising general court-martial jurisdiction (OEGCMJ) over the 
complained-of commanding officer. Before submitting an Article 138 complaint, 
members must request in writing that their commanding officer redress the wrong. The 
OEGCMJ must act upon this request for redress in a timely manner (ordinarily within 
30 days) and notify the complainant in writing of the action taken. The Article 138 
complaint may address a broad range of wrongs, but does not include issues covered 
by a specific appeal process, such as evaluations, non-judicial punishment, and court-
martial. Article 138, UCMJ. 
A.2. Wrong Any act, omission, decision or order, except those excluded by Subsection 25.B.2., 
taken, caused, or ratified by a commanding officer, under color of that officer’s military 
authority, that (1) results in personal detriment, harm, or injury to a military subordinate; 
and (2) is without statutory or regulatory basis, unauthorized, an abuse of discretion, 
arbitrary and capricious, unjust or discriminatory. 
A.3 Commanding officer  For purposes of an Article 138 complaint, “commanding officer” means a commissioned 
or warrant officer who, by virtue of rank, assignment, or both, exercises primary 
command authority over a military organization, or prescribed territorial area, to which 
the complainant is assigned, that under official directives is recognized as a “command.” 
See Manual for Courts-Martial, Part V. Para. 2a. 
A.4. Officer exercising 
general court-martial 
jurisdiction (OEGCMJ) 
The primary OEGCMJ is the OEGCMJ for the unit of the commanding officer about 
whom the complaint is filed. 
A.5. Respondent  The individual who committed the alleged wrong(s) against the complainant, and against 
whom the complaint of wrongs is made. The respondent may be a uniformed member 
of any armed service. 
A.6. Complainant Member submitting complaint. 
A.7. Redress Any lawful action by the commanding officer, the OEGCMCJ, or any officer in the 
chain of command that restores the complainant any rights, privileges, property, or status 
he or she would have been entitled to had the wrong not occurred.

administrative 
regularity

Section B. Policy 
It is the policy of the Secretary, Department of Homeland Security (DHS) to resolve 
complaints of wrongs at the lowest possible level. There is a presumption of 
administrative regularity. A commanding officer must not restrict the submission of 
complaints under Article 138, UCMJ, and all such complaints must be forwarded 
directly to the responsible OEGCMJ over the complainant. 
The following actions are not proper subjects of a complaint of wrongs: 
a. Acts, omissions, decisions and orders not taken, caused or ratified by 
respondent; 
b. Acts that are not final;

c. General policies of the Department of Homeland Security or the Coast Guard, 
including the Instructions and other documents promulgating such policies; 
d. The OEGCMJ’s decisions and procedures on complaints of wrongs, except for 
failure to forward the complaint; 
e. Proceedings, findings, or final actions of nonjudicial punishment (NJP), court-
martial, and officer, enlisted, and cadet administrative discharge procedures 
and results; 
f. Actions regarding officer fitness reports; 
g. Actions regarding enlisted performance evaluation marks; 
h. Actions in accordance with the Enlistments, Evaluations and Advancement 
Manual, COMDTINST M1000.2, regarding withdrawal removal of designator 
or administrative reductions in enlisted grade; 
i. Appeal from findings of pecuniary liability; 
j. Withdrawal of flying status; 
k. Military civil rights complaints under the Coast Guard Civil Rights Manual, 
COMDTINST M5350.4(series); and 
l. Reliefs for cause of commanding officers and officers-in-charge. 
B.3. Improper complaint Action against or on behalf of another person, such as court-martial charges, NJP, or 
apologies, may not be requested to a redress a wrong under this Chapter.

C.3.a. General rule  
C.3.b. Reassignment of

Section C. Participants to a Complaint Process 
At the time the complaint is submitted, the complainant must be: 
a. A member of the armed forces on activ e duty, concerning a wrong which is 
alleged to have occurred while the complainant was on active duty; or 
b. Any reservist, concerning a wrong alleged to have been committed by the 
respondent acting in his or her official capacity as a commanding officer. 
A complaint must be against a specific person, not a command or position. Any 
commanding officer, as defined in Subsection 25.A.3, may be the respondent to a 
complaint of wrongs. The Secretary of Homeland Security may not be a respondent to 
a complaint of wrongs. 
The OEGCMJ has the primary res ponsibility to conduct an inquiry into the complaint 
of wrongs, take action on it, and submit a report of the proceedings to the Commandant 
through the Office of Military Justice (CG-LMJ). 
If the complainant, respondent, or both, detach prior to the submission of a complaint of 
wrongs, the complaint will be forwarded to the OEGCMJ over the respondent at the 
time of the alleged wrong, through the co mplainant’s current commanding officer and 
the respondent. 
Where the complainant is assigned to a joint command, and the OEGCMJ over the 
respondent is a member of another service, the complaint is forwarded to the OEGCMJ 
through the senior Coast Guard officer in the joint command. If the OEGCMJ 
determines that the complaint raises issues unique to the Coast Guard and addressable 
only under Coast Guard Instructions, the complaint is forwarded to the Coast Guard 
OEGCMJ overseeing the activity or joint command at issue. 
An intermediate superior officer, subordin ate to the OEGCMJ, but superior to the 
respondent, to whom a complaint is forwarded, may comment on the merits of the 
complaint, add pertinent evidence, and, if empowered to do so, grant redress, noting

such action on the record. In all cases, i mmediate superior officers must promptly 
forward the complaint to the OEGCMJ, and provide a copy of any comments, 
endorsement, or redress. If a complainan t withdraws a complaint, per Subsection 
25.D.9., the record of the complaint and withdrawal will be forwarded to the OEGCMJ 
who must maintain a record of the complaint.

source: Coast Guard Military Justice Manual (COMDTINST M5810.1H)
text:
e superior officers must promptly 
forward the complaint to the OEGCMJ, and provide a copy of any comments, 
endorsement, or redress. If a complainan t withdraws a complaint, per Subsection 
25.D.9., the record of the complaint and withdrawal will be forwarded to the OEGCMJ 
who must maintain a record of the complaint.

Section D. Submission Procedure

D.1. Initial request for 
redress prior to 
submitting a complaint 
Before a complainant may submit a complaint of wrongs under Article 138, UCMJ, the 
complainant must request, in writing, that the Commanding Officer redress the wrong. 
The Commanding Officer must act upon this request for redress in a timely manner, 
ordinarily within 30 days, and notify the complainant in writing of the action taken.

D.2. Time Limitations A complaint must be submitted within a reasonable time after discovery of the alleged 
wrong. Absent unusual circumstances, a complaint submitted more than 90 days after 
the complainant discovers the alleged wrong is untimely. The period during which the 
commanding officer is considering the complainant’s written request for redress under 
Subsection 25.D.1 is not included in this 90-day period. The OEGCMJ may deny relief 
solely because the complaint is untimely. If, however, th e OEGCMJ determines that 
unusual circumstances justify the delay in submission, the OEGCMJ may find that the 
complaint is timely and act on it.

D.3. Form of complaint The complaint must be couched in temperate language and be confined to pertinent facts 
, and contain the following information:

a. The specific wrongs alleged and the specific redress requested, followed 
thereafter by explanatory information;

b. All relevant evidence, including affidavits, statements, and documents with the 
complaint as numbered Enclosures.

D.4. Joinder A complaint may not be joined with the complaints of other individuals. Similarly, each 
complainant may seek redress for the wr ong(s) of only one respondent. If the 
complainant believes more than one respondent has committed a wrong, the complainant 
must submit a separate complaint against each respondent, not against a group such as 
“the chain of command.”

D.5. Forwarding the 
complaint  
The complainant must forward the complaint to the OEGCMJ, through his or her current 
chain of command, and to the respondent.

D.6. Endorsements Intermediate endorsers should ordinarily forward the complaint within 10 working days 
after receipt. Endorsements not completed w ithin 10 working days of receipt must 
contain an explanation for the delay. Subject to applicable security of classified material 
Instructions, endorsers must provide to th e complainant copies of their endorsement, 
including enclosures.

source: Coast Guard Military Justice Manual (COMDTINST M5810.1H)
text:
e complaint within 10 working days 
after receipt. Endorsements not completed w ithin 10 working days of receipt must 
contain an explanation for the delay. Subject to applicable security of classified material 
Instructions, endorsers must provide to th e complainant copies of their endorsement, 
including enclosures.

D.7. Respondent’s 
response 
The respondent must respond, in writing, to the complaint of wrongs and forward that 
response, along with the complaint and any endorsements, to the OEGCMJ within 10 
working days after receipt of the complaint. If the response is not completed within 10 
working days of receipt, the response must explain the delay. Subject to applicable 
security of classified material Instructions, the respondent must provide the complainant 
with copies of his or her response, including enclosures. If a complaint of wrongs is 
received by the OEGCMJ without a response from the respondent, it must be forwarded 
to the respondent to provide a written response back to the OEGCMJ, with a copy to the 
complainant, prior to being considered by the OEGCMJ.

D.8. Complainant’s  Subject to applicable security of classified material Instructions, the OEGCMJ must: 
notifications and a.  Ensure that the complainant has been  provided a copy of all materials (to  
rebuttal include, but not limited to, the Respondent’s response and any substantive 
endorsements) received, as well as any evidence developed by the 
OEGCMJ’s inquiry; and

b.  Notify the complainant of the opportunity to  rebut any matter contained  
therein within  10 working days of receipt of the notice.

D.8.a. Opportunity to  No opportunity to rebut is required  where the OEGCMJ returns a defective or improper 
rebut  complaint to the complainant pursuant to  Subsection 25.E.2.  The complainant may  
submit any rebuttal to the OEGCMJ, through the commanding o  fficer and the 
respondent. No further opportunity to rebut  is required where the respondent or  
intermediate endorsers provided plain endorsements on the rebuttal, for example, 
“forwarded,” “forwarded for action as  appropriate,” or “f orwarded recommending  
denial.” However, complainant must be provided a copy of such endorsements.

D.8.b. Rebuttal The OEGCMJ m ust provide a copy of the rebuttal notification and the rebuttal as 
documentation   enclosures to the final report. See Section 25.F. If the complainant declines to  provide  
rebuttal or fails to provide  rebuttal within 10 working days, the final report must  
adequately document the declination  or failure.

D.9. Withdrawal   A complainant may withdraw a complaint at  any time. The withdrawal must be in 
writing and signed by the complainant.

D.10. Waiver   The OEGCMJ may waive any requirement in th is Section, except those afforded to  
benefit a complainant, such as the right to receive copies of all materials received and to  
rebut any matters submitted to or discovered  by the OEGCMJ or any intermediate 
endorsers.

D.10. Waiver   The OEGCMJ may waive any requirement in th is Section, except those afforded to  
benefit a complainant, such as the right to receive copies of all materials received and to  
rebut any matters submitted to or discovered  by the OEGCMJ or any intermediate 
endorsers.

Section E. Review of the Complaint by  the OEGCMJ 
  
E.1 Review   The OEGCMJ must review the complaint to  ensure that:

a.  The complainant has requested  redress from the respondent;

b.  The complaint is timely;

c.  The complaint is complete;

d.  The complaint does not  join  more than  one complaint or more than  one  
respondent;

e.  The complaint has been  properly forwarded;

f.  The respondent has responded to the complaint in  writing;

g.  The alleged wrong is a wrong and a proper subject  of a complaint of wrongs;  
and

h.  The requested redress is proper.

E.2. De
fective or 
improper complaints 
If the complaint is defective in that it fails to satisfy the requirements of Subsections 
Subsection 25.C.1 (who may complain) or 25.C.2 (who may be the subject of a 
complaint), does not allege a wrong that is a proper subject of a complaint of wrongs, or 
makes no proper request for relief, the OEGCMJ must return the defective complaint to 
the complainant with an explanation as to why it is outside the scope of this Chapter. In 
returning a complaint, if appropriate, the OEGCMJ should inform the complainant about 
other channels available to resolve the alleged wrong. If the complaint is incomplete or 
otherwise fails to satisfy the procedural requirements of Subsections 25.D.1-4, the 
OEGCMJ must, unless the deficiency is waiv ed under Subsection 25.D.10, return the 
complaint to the complainant with an explanation. In returning the complaint, the 
OEGCMJ must not address the merits of the complaint.

E.3. Inquiry If the complaint is not improper or defective, the OEGCMJ  must inquire into the merits  
of the allegation(s). The extent and nature of such inquiry is within the OEGCMJ’s 
discretion, and depends on the seriousness of the allegations, the extent  of the  
investigation conducted by the chain of  command subordinate to the OEGCMJ, the 
available time, and the exigencies of operations. The OEGCMJ may appoint an 
investigating  officer to inquire into the complaint. The OEGCMJ may also request the 
complainant, the respondent, or  both submit additional explanatory statements or  other 
relevant documents.

E.4. Complainant’s Prior to taking  final action  on  a complaint, and  subject to applicable security of classified  
rebuttal and material Instructions, the OEGCMJ must:  
notifications   a.  Ensure compliance with Subsection  25.D.8;

b.  Ensure the complainant has been provided a copy  of any new matters 
developed by the OEGCMJ inquiry; and

source: Coast Guard Military Justice Manual (COMDTINST M5810.1H)

text:
taking  final action  on  a complaint, and  subject to applicable security of classified  
rebuttal and material Instructions, the OEGCMJ must:  
notifications   a.  Ensure compliance with Subsection  25.D.8;

b.  Ensure the complainant has been provided a copy  of any new matters 
developed by the OEGCMJ inquiry; and

c.  Inform the complainant of the opportunity to rebut any new matter within 10  
working days.  
The complainant must submit any new rebuttal materials to the OEGCMJ through the 
complainant’s commanding officer and the respondent. As with the original complaint, 
if the respondent or intermediate endorsers provide anything more than plain 
endorsements, for example, “forwarded,” “f orwarded for action as appropriate,” or 
“forwarded recommending denial,” in  forwarding the rebuttal, the OEGCMJ must  
ensure the complainant is pr ovided a copy of all materials received and inform the 
complainant of the opportunity to rebut any matters contained therein.

E.5. Time limitations  Except in  unusual circumstances, the OEGCMJ must act on the complaint within  90 
days of receipt. If the OEGCMJ action is not completed within 90 days of receipt, the  
action must explain the delay.

E.6. Pers onal action by  The OEGCMJ  must take personal action on the complaint. The OEGCMJ  must not  
the OEGCMJ delegate action to a subordinate command or individual.

E.7. Standard of proof Facts must be established in the OEGCMJ’s determination  by a “preponderance of the 
evidence.”

E.8. Redress  The following provides guidance to the OEGCMJ:

a.  If the OEGCMJ determines the complaint is without merit, the OEGCMJ must  
deny redress;

b.  If the OEGCMJ determines the complaint has merit, the OEGCMJ must grant  
such redress as is appropriate and with in the OEGCMJ’s authority. In certain 
situations, a complaint may have merit; however, redress may not be  available, 
appropriate, or proper within the context of a complaint of wrongs. In such 
cases the OEGCMJ should acknowledge th e merit of the complaint, even if  
relief is denied; and

c.  If the OEGCMJ determines the complaint has merit, but cannot actually effect  
the appropriate redress (i.e.,  removal  of  a fitness report  or  performance  
evaluation from a service record), the OEGCMJ must forward the record to an  
officer who can effect it, requesting that the specific relief be granted. That 
officer must effect the relief, unless  the officer determines that the relief  
requested is not permitted by  current regulations or is otherwise prohibited  by  
law or  policy.

officer who can effect it, requesting that the specific relief be granted. That 
officer must effect the relief, unless  the officer determines that the relief  
requested is not permitted by  current regulations or is otherwise prohibited  by  
law or  policy.

Section F. Forwarding OEGCMJ Action  
  
F.1. Written response to The OEGCMJ must advise the complainant in  writing  of the action  taken  on the 
the complainant  complaint. The letter must specifically indicate which  of the complainant's allegations

F.2. Forwarding the 
OEGCMJ report 
F.3. Signature of 
OEGCMJ 
have merit and which are without merit and must either specify the relief granted or 
expressly deny the requested relief. 
In all cases, the OEGCMJ must prepare an d forward to the Commandant, through the 
Judge Advocate General (the Office of Military Justice (CG-LMJ)) a report on the 
complaint. In cases where Commandant, U. S. Coast Guard is the respondent, the 
Secretary of Homeland Security provides review and makes final disposition. The report 
must include the complaint with all endorsements and Enclosures, a copy of any relevant 
correspondence with the complainant or respondent including any rebuttal notifications 
and rebuttals, and a copy of any pertinent investigations that were used in the decision 
and had been provided to the complainant. The basic letter should simply state “Per 
references (a) and (b), forwarded for final review.” No further elaboration is required 
(as any elaboration may require forwarding back to the complainant for review). 
The report submitted to the Commandant and the response to the complainant, if by 
separate correspondence, must be signed personally by the OEGCMJ, or, in that officer's 
absence, by the officer offici ally acting in such capacity, with the signature block so 
indicating.

Section G. Action by the Judge Advocate General and Secretary  
G.1. Ensure compliance 
with Article 138, UCMJ 
G.1.a. Substantial 
compliance 
G.1.b. No substantial 
compliance  
G.2. Action by the 
Secretary 
When a complaint has been acted upon by OEGCMJ, upon receipt of the OEGCMJ 's 
report, the Judge Advocate General must ensure that there has been substantial 
compliance with Article 138, UCMJ. Responsibility for review for compliance is with 
the Office of Military Justice (CG-LMJ) on behalf of the Judge Advocate General. 
If there has been substantial compliance, th e Office of Military Justice (CG-LMJ) will 
review the complaint and action. If the Ju dge Advocate General agrees with the action 
taken, the complaint will be filed for the Commandant in the Office of Military Justice 
(CG-LMJ). If the Judge Advocat e General disagrees with the action taken, the Judge 
Advocate General will forward the file to the Secretary, recommending a different 
action. 
If there has not been substantial compliance, the Office of Military Justice (CG-LMJ) 
will notify the Judge Advocate General and return the file to the OEGCMJ for additional 
investigation or further action. 
If the Secretary, in his or her discretion, takes action on a complaint, the complainant, 
respondent, and OEGCMJ will be informed.

text:
Certification and Designation of Judges

Section A. Authority and Responsibility of the Coast Guard Chief Trial Judge  
 A.1. Authority and 
responsibility 
The Chief Trial Judge shall exercise superv isory authority over the Coast Guard Trial 
Judiciary. The Chief Trial Judge shall ove rsee and administer the Coast Guard Trial 
Judiciary in the fair and timely rendering of judicial services, supervise and coordinate 
the activities of all judiciary personnel, issue comprehensive standard operating 
procedures, ensure the effective interchange of information and services among military 
judges, liaise with other se rvice Chief Trial Judges, ensure the effective accession 
training and continuing judicial education for all trial judges, and perform other duties 
as may be directed by The Judge Advocate General of the Coast Guard (TJAG). By this 
instruction, TJAG delegates to the Chief Trial Judge the authority to carry out the full 
range of duties related to the administration of the Coast Guard Trial Judiciary.

source: Coast Guard Military Justice Manual (COMDTINST M5810.1H)

icial education for all trial judges, and perform other duties 
as may be directed by The Judge Advocate General of the Coast Guard (TJAG). By this 
instruction, TJAG delegates to the Chief Trial Judge the authority to carry out the full 
range of duties related to the administration of the Coast Guard Trial Judiciary.

Section B. Role, Selection, Certification, and Qualification of Coast Guard Military Judges

B.1. Role of Coast Coast Guard trial judges preside over special and general courts-martial and pre-referral 
Guard military trial judicial proceedings. In doing so, they have a duty to ensure that trial proceedings are 
judges  conducted in a fair, orderl y, transparent, and efficient manner, enhance public 
confidence in the Coast Guard’s military justice system, and develop new judge 
advocates and legal yeomen serving in courts-martial.

B.2. How Coast Guard Judge Advocates are selected , certified under Article 26(b), UCMJ, designated, and 
military judges are assigned as military judges by TJAG. TJAG designates both full-time military judges 
selected and certified  (including the Chief Trial Judge of the Coast Guard) and collateral duty military judges.

B.3. Qualifications TJAG selects the best qualified judge advocates for both full-time and collateral-duty 
military judge assignments using the following factors:

a. Prior certification as counsel. Candidates must be certified under Article 27(b), 
UCMJ.  
Military justice experience. Candidates must have at least four years in criminal 
or civil litigation billets (e.g. trial or defense counsel, special victim counsel, 
Appellate litigation, Special Assistant U.S. Attorney). Experience trying cases 
in civilian jurisdictions, is helpful, but generally not solely sufficient for 
qualification. Candidates should describe in detail their military justice and trial 
experience, to include number of trials  completed in each role or capacity, 
noting whether such trials were contested, whether they were before members, 
and any particularly complex or signi ficant issues that arose during the 
trial(s).Candidates should also detail a ppellate practice experience, to include 
number and complexity of briefs prepared and oral arguments made. Education 
and training, both at continuing legal education courses and degree programs 
such as LLM programs in trial advocacy, and national publications on military 
justice topics, are also factors in determining whether candidates have the 
requisite experience and in distinguishing those best-qualified.

b. Paygrade. Candidates for full-time billets must be in the pay grade of O-5 or 
above.  Candidates for collateral duty billets must be in pay grade O-4 or above.

c. Availability to serve. Candidates for collateral-duty military judge assignment 
must be available to serve as a military  judge at four trials or pre-referral 
judicial proceedings per year for three years following certification. They must 
also be available to attend the annual five-day Joint Military Judiciary Annual 
Training (JMJAT), and to complete a certificate program through the National 
Judicial College (NJC). In addition, collateral duty trial judges must be 
prepared to be on call to review sear ch authorizations in accordance with 
policies determined by the Chief Trial Judge.

B.3.a. Disqualifying duty 
assignments

B.4. Application 
procedures

d. Prior performance . The candidate’s Headquarters Personnel Data Record 
(PDR) will be reviewed.

B.4. Application 
procedures

d. Prior performance . The candidate’s Headquarters Personnel Data Record 
(PDR) will be reviewed.

e. Other information . Any relevant information provided from other sources, 
including but not limited to the comments of judges the applicant has practiced 
before, professional reputation within the Coast Guard, and performance 
information submitted by the applicant that is not contained in the PDR. 
Information from other sources may be disclosed to the applicant, upon request. 
The number of applicants to be recommended in a given year will depend on the 
projected needs of the service for replacements of military judges expected to become 
unavailable. 
Those currently serving in a full-time military justice capacity, such as trial or appellate 
defense or government counsel, or Special Victims’ Counsel, and those serving in a 
command position designated as a special court-martial convening authority, and 
assignments to Naval Justice School, the Army Judge Advocate General’s Legal Center 
and School, and the Air Force Judge Advocate General’s School will not be designated 
as military judges while serving in those capacities. Those currently serving in 
“Detached Duty – External” positions such as  fellows, liaisons, or similar billets will 
not be designated as military judges if the Chief Trial Judge determines service in those 
billets would be incompatible with promoting confidence in the integrity and 
impartiality of the judiciary. 
Coast Guard judge advocates desiring to be  certified as a full-time or collateral duty 
military trial judge must apply, through their chain of command, and the Chief Trial 
Judge (CG-094J), to the Office of Legal Policy and Program Development (CG-LPD), 
no later than 31 January annually. The application will include the following 
information:

a. Education. Include all education after high school (with names of institutions 
attended and year of graduation) including academic distinctions attained and 
approximate (if not exactly known) place in class on graduation.

b. Military experience. List all military assignments, including primary duties at 
each.

c. Dates. List dates designated a judge ad vocate and certified as counsel for 
general courts-martial.

d. Military justice experience . State, in detail, all past experience with military 
justice, both prior and subsequent to being designated a law specialist. Be as 
specific as possible in the number, forum, type, and level of review of records 
of trial. See Subsection 26.B.3.

e. Legal experience other than military justice. Identify all prior legal experience 
that did not involve military justice. This will include experience gained prior 
to becoming a member of the Coast Guard, all non-legal assignments 
significantly law related, and all assignments in legal billets, delineating the 
primary, nonmilitary justice areas of the law dealt with as required by the billet.

f. Evidence of requisite knowledge and temperament to be a military judge . 
Applicants must provide two letters of recommendation from senior judge 
advocates who have had a significant opportunity to observe the applicant’s 
military justice practice, judgement, temperament, maturity, leadership, 
mentoring, and writing skills. At least one of these recommendations must be 
from a current or former military or civilian judge before whom the applicant 
practiced. If the applicant cannot obtain such an appraisal from a military or 
civilian judge, the applicant must clearly  address the reason for its absence in 
his or her application.  Judicial temperament includes, but is not limited to, 
patience, forbearance to avoid premature decisions, calm demeanor, respect for 
others, and projection of an air of authority.

g. Statement of Availability.  A statement concerning th e applicant’s expected 
availability to serve as military judge for the three years following the next

h. 
Military Judge Course. The statement should cover both the likelihood of 
remaining in a billet from which the applicant could periodically be spared and 
any special restrictions on availability within the billet (e.g., any assignment 
that precludes military judge service during the months of July and August). 
Authorization. A PDR Review Authorization enclosure signed and dated by the 
applicant, coordinated by Personnel Service Command.

B.5. Selection The Chief Trial Judge is solely responsible, in consultation with the Office of Legal 
Policy and Program Development (CG-LPD), for reviewing applications for 
certification, prepare a recommendation, and forward it to TJAG. TJAG will select the 
best qualified applicants, who will be assigned to attend the Military Judge Course. CG-
LPD will notify the applicants of the results. TJAG will certify the new judges after they 
successfully complete the Military Judge Course.

Section C. Designation as Military Judge  
C.1. General court-
martial military judges  
General court-martial military judges are designated, and located at Coast Guard units, 
as determined by TJAG. Administrative an d logistics support, including office space, 
office equipment, stationery , and office supplies, telephone and other communication 
services, access to law library, and clerical assistance, will be provided by the unit where 
the military judge is located. 
C.2. Collateral duty 
special court-martial 
military judges 
In addition to full-time general court-martial judges, TJAG may also designate collateral 
duty judges to serve as special court-martial military trial judges. 
C.3. Oath A military judge certified in accordance with Article 26(b), UCMJ, may take a one-time 
oath to perform his or her duties faithfully and impartially in all cases to which detailed. 
The oath may be taken at any time and may be administered by any officer authorized 
by Article 136, UCMJ, to administer oaths. Once such an oath is taken, the military 
judge need not be re-sworn at any court-martial to which subsequently detailed. 
C.4. Officer evaluation 
reports  
TJAG is the supervisor, reporting officer, and reviewer for the Chief Trial Judge. The 
Chief Trial Judge is the supervisor and reporting officer for all other general court-
martial military Judges. TJAG as the Reviewer must provide a Coast Guard form 5315 
(CG-5315) for all active military trial and appellate judges. See Ch. 5.D.2, Officer 
Accessions, Evaluations and Promotions, COMDTINST M1000.3A.

heading_path: Duties of the Coast Guard Trial Judiciary
text:
he 
Chief Trial Judge is the supervisor and reporting officer for all other general court-
martial military Judges. TJAG as the Reviewer must provide a Coast Guard form 5315 
(CG-5315) for all active military trial and appellate judges. See Ch. 5.D.2, Officer 
Accessions, Evaluations and Promotions, COMDTINST M1000.3A.

Section D. Duties of the Coast Guard Trial Judiciary 
D.1. General court-
martial judges 
D.2. Collateral duty 
special courts-marital 
military judges 
The primary duty of general court-martial military judges is to preside at general courts-
martial. No person may assign them any duties other than that of military judge without 
prior notification of the Chief Trial Judg e and authorization of TJAG. TJAG has 
determined that general court-martial military  judges will be made available for detail 
as military judge for special courts-martial or pre-referral judicial proceedings on a not-
to-interfere basis with their primary duty. TJAG approves leave and temporary duty for 
the Chief Trial Judge. The Chief Trial Judge approves leave and temporary duty for the 
other general court-martial military judges. 
The primary duty of collateral duty special-court-martial military judges is normally in 
accordance with their assigned permanent b illets. However, when detailed to preside 
over a special court-martial, the military judge must perform this duty fully and 
expeditiously, and is responsible for balancing his or her primary duties to accomplish 
this. Primary duty supervisors of collateral-dut y military judges should be aware that 
duty as a special court-martial military judge may need to be considered equivalent to 
the officer’s primary duties.

D.3. Military Magistrate The Chief Trial Judge is authorized, at his or her discretion, to create a military 
Program magistrate program in accordance with 10 U.S.C. §826a and RCM. 502(c)(2). If 
established, the military magistrate program may be altered or dissolved by TJAG or the 
Chief Trial Judge.

Section E. Detailing Military Judges to Courts-Martial or Pre-Referral Judicial Proceedings 
E.1.  Generally The Chief Trial Judge is authorized to detail military judges for courts-martial or pre-
referral judicial proceedings. This authority may be delegated to an alternate docketing 
judge. Nothing in this regulation precludes the detail of a military judge from another 
armed force who has been made available for detail, provided that such military judge 
has been certified by the Judge Advocate Gene ral of the military judge’s armed force. 
Coast Guard military judges may preside at courts-martial of other armed forces, under 
RCM 503(b)(3) if permitted by the Chief Trial Judge. To aid in the detailing of judges 
to courts-martial or pre-referral judicial proceedings, the Chief Trial Judge is authorized 
to establish judicial circuits. If established, judicial circuits may be altered or dissolved 
by TJAG or the Chief Trial Judge.

Section F. Assignment Lengths for Military Trial Judges 
F.1. General court- A General Court-Martial military trial judge, including the Chief Trial Judge, is assigned 
martial judges for a minimum of three years, except when he or she: 
a. Retires or otherwise separates from military service; 
b. Is assigned elsewhere to meet exceptional needs of the service; or, 
c. Is temporarily, indefinitely, or permanently suspended from practice as a 
military trial judge by TJAG for good cause. 
F.2. Collateral duty A collateral duty military trial judge is assigned for a minimum of three years and may 
special court-marital request to serve beyond the normal three-year term. Requests may be made to the Chief 
military judges Trial Judge by no later than 1 February of the year in which the normal three-year term 
ends. The minimum 3-year term does not a pply whenever the Sp ecial Court-Martial 
Judge: 
a. Assumes duty as a trial judge on a less th an three-year basis or requests to be 
relieved of trial judge duties, and TJAG approves such assignment; 
b. Retires or otherwise separates from military service; 
c. Is reassigned to another billet, under the normal personnel assignment process 
based on the needs of the service and without regard to any prior performance 
of judicial duties, which billet TJAG determines is incompatible with duty as a 
military trial judge; or, 
Is temporarily, indefinitely, or permanently suspended from practice as a military trial 
judge by TJAG for good cause.

Section G. Appellate Judges 
TJAG assigns judges to the Coast Guard Court of Criminal Appeals, including 
collateral-duty judges. TJAG designates one of the assigned judges as Chief Appellate 
Judge.  Persons interested in becoming a collateral duty appellate judge should contact 
the chief of CG-LPD or the Chief Appellate  Judge.  Candidates will be required to 
provide a resume showing their military justice experience. 
Coast Guard appellate judges serve on the Co ast Guard Court of Cr iminal Appeals as 
provided in Article 66, UCMJ. Pursuant to Article 66(a), UCMJ, and RCM 1203(a), 
TJAG certifies Judge Advocates or civilian attorneys as qualified to serve as appellate 
judges by reason of education, training, e xperience, and judicial temperament.  Unless 
waived by TJAG, prospective appellate judges must successfully complete a formal

course of instruction specifically designed to  prepare a Judge Advo cate to serve as an 
appellate judge.

G.3. Chief Appellate 
Judge 
The position of the Chief Judge of the Coast Guard Court of Criminal Appeals is a 
designated civilian position. The Chief Judge remains in the position until he or she:

a. Retires or otherwise separates from Coast Guard service;

b. Accepts another position, under normal federal civilian employment processes; 
or, 
Is temporarily, indefinitely, or permanently suspend from practice as an appellate judge 
by TJAG for good cause.

G.4. Full-time Appellate 
judges 
Full-time judges of the Coast Guard Court of Criminal Appeals may be military or 
civilian. Civilian Judges of the Coast Guard Court of Criminal Appeals are appointed 
by the Secretary, and serve indefinitely, with the same exceptions as appear in 
Subsection 26.G.2. A full-time military judge  is assigned primary duty to the Coast 
Guard Court of Criminal Appeals for a minimum of three years, except when he or she:

a. Assumed the duty as an appellate judge on a less than three year basis; or 
requests to be relieved of duty as appellate judge, and TJAG approves such a 
request;

b. Retires or otherwise separates from Coast Guard service;

c. Is reassigned to another billet under the normal personnel assignment process 
based on the needs of the service and without regard to any prior performance 
of judicial duties; or, 
Is temporarily, indefinitely, or permanently suspended from practice as an appellate 
judge by TJAG for good cause.

G.5. Collateral duty 
appellate judges  
An appellate judge is assigned to collateral duty on the Coast Guard Court of Criminal 
Appeals for a minimum of three years, except when he or she:

a. Assumed the duty as an appellate judge on a less than three year basis or 
requests to be relieved of duty as appellate judge, and TJAG approves such 
request;

b. Retires or otherwise separates from Coast Guard or DHS service;
