section: Chapter 6 -- Testing for Controlled and Prohibited Substances
chapter_title: Testing for Controlled and Prohibited Substances
heading_path: Testing for Controlled and Prohibited Substances
text:
A. Objectives. 
Education and Prevention. Enhance mission-readiness and foster an environment free 
from controlled and prohibited substances through a comprehensive program of 
education, prevention, and deterrence. 
Detection and Deterrance. Establish and maintain a reliable substance inspection and 
testing program to detect and deter the use of controlled and other prohibited substances. 
A robust drug inspection program maintains operational and personnel readiness 
preserving the safety and security of the total Coast Guard workforce. This is best 
achieved through a reliable means of random and frequent substance inspection and 
testing of Coast Guard military members. 
Data Collection. Produce data to measure controlled and prohibited substance use 
incidents among Coast Guard military members. Urinalysis and additional drug testing 
are examples of common practices to detect and deter wrongful substance use and, as 
such, units must conduct frequent and random testing throughout the FY in accordance 
with FY Drug Test Allocations guidance messages published by the All Coast Guard 
Personnel Service Center (ALCGPSC).

source: CG Military Substance Abuse and Behavioral Addiction Program (COMDTINST 1000.10B)
section: Chapter 6 -- Testing for Controlled and Prohibited Substances
chapter_title: Testing for Controlled and Prohibited Substances
heading_path: Testing for Controlled and Prohibited Substances
text:
is and additional drug testing 
are examples of common practices to detect and deter wrongful substance use and, as 
such, units must conduct frequent and random testing throughout the FY in accordance 
with FY Drug Test Allocations guidance messages published by the All Coast Guard 
Personnel Service Center (ALCGPSC).

B. Types of Substances. For the purposes of this Instruction, there are two types of substances 
the use of which may subject a Coast Guard member to administrative discipline or criminal 
punishment under the UCMJ: (1) a controlled substance or drug, and (2) a prohibited 
substance. 
Drug/Controlled Substance. A drug or a controlled substance is a substance or immediate 
precursor that has potential for misuse, abuse, or dependence as defined in 21 U.S.C. § 
802(6) and (12) or referenced in the UCMJ (10 U.S.C. § 912a), Article 112a. 
Prohibited Substance. A prohibited substance is a substance that does not fall under the 
definition of a drug or a controlled substance in 21 U.S.C. § 802(6) and (12) but is 
prohibited from use by policy. This encompasses, but is not limited to, the inhalation, 
injection, consumption, application to skin, or other introduction to the body of any mood 
altering substance or compound, existing now or created in the future, including: 
a. Analogues of controlled or synthetic-substance, for example, designer drugs invented 
or yet to have been composed, such as products containing synthetic cannabinoids, 
synthetic marijuana, synthetic cannabidiol (CBD), spice, fentanyl, that are not 
otherwise controlled substances; 
b. Substances that are not or no longer controlled but pose a risk of consumption, 
6-1

absorption, or otherwise induction into the body an excessive amount of a controlled 
substance, such as performance-enhancing drugs explicitly listed in Classes S1, S2 
and S4 of the World Anti-Doping Code Prohibited List and tetrahydrocannabinol 
(THC). Such substances include, but are not limited to, hemp and hemp-derived CBD 
including Delta-8, Delta-9, and Delta-10 THC as defined in Paragraph 7.A.2, 
Marijuana and Tetrahydrocannabinol (THC); 
c. Inhalants; 
d. Propellants; 
e. Solvents; 
f. Household chemicals; 
g. Substances used for huffing; 
h. Prescription or OTC medications in excess of prescribed dosage; and, 
i. Any naturally occurring intoxicating substances, such as, Salvia divinorum or 
products containing Salvia divinorum.

source: CG Military Substance Abuse and Behavioral Addiction Program (COMDTINST 1000.10B)
section: Chapter 6 -- Testing for Controlled and Prohibited Substances

hydrocannabinol (THC); 
c. Inhalants; 
d. Propellants; 
e. Solvents; 
f. Household chemicals; 
g. Substances used for huffing; 
h. Prescription or OTC medications in excess of prescribed dosage; and, 
i. Any naturally occurring intoxicating substances, such as, Salvia divinorum or 
products containing Salvia divinorum.

C. Roles and Responsibilities. 
Commander (CG-PSC-PSD-FS). The Program Manager (PM) has overall responsibility 
for the maintenance and success of the Coast Guard Drug Testing Program. The PM has 
authority and responsibility to:

a. Establish and verify that the Coast Guard is maintaining a robust program capable of 
identifying current and future needs. The PM must develop and sustain training to 
properly qualify personnel to perform their duties throughout the drug testing process;

b. Develop and sustain on-hand reference material focused on testing procedures, such 
as desk guides and checklists, to reduce discrepancies and untestable samples;

c. Maintain normal communications with the MAJCOMs to include allocating 
members/units geographically and determining fleet-wide trends; and,

d. Maintain records of pertinent correspondence with the DoD Forensic Toxicology 
Drug Testing Laboratory (FTDTL) facility and WebDTP access documents for all 
MAJCOM/DTC/ADTCs, including but not limited to System Authorization Access 
Request (SAAR) Form DD-2875 series, designation memos, training documentation, 
and other documentation necessary to record a unit’s program. 
Commanding Officers (COs) and Officers-in-Charge (OICs). COs/OICs have authority 
and responsibility to: 
6-2

a. Direct their unit members to submit to a unit’s Drug Testing Program;

b. Enforce the provisions of this Instruction based upon the findings of such urinalysis, 
blood test, HST, OFT, or other approved additional drug tests;

c. Contract with a DoD or Department of Health and Human Services (DHHS) certified 
laboratory for HST, OFT, or steroid and controlled performance enhancing drug 
testing;

d. Notify MAJCOMs and local CGIS office within 24 hours from report of a positive 
OFT, HST, or steroid test;

e. Monitor the CGBI Drug Testing Report to ensure that all unit personnel (100 percent 
assigned) are urinalysis tested at least once during the FY, in accordance with 
Paragraph 6.D.1.d., Random Selection Testing Requirements, of this Instruction; and,

f. Ensure all positive results are reported and processed according to this Instruction and 
the procedures on the CG-PSC-PSD-FS CG portal page at 
https://cg.portal.uscg.mil/units/psc/FS/default.aspx. 
Major Drug Testing Coordinator (MAJCOMs). MAJCOMs serve as an important conduit 
of information and oversight linking individual units to the Coast Guard Drug Testing 
Program. MAJCOMs provide critical supervision and advisement for the drug testing 
program, therefore MAJCOM will have the rank of E-7/GS-09 or above. If the 
MAJCOM is designated for multiple drug testing roles, the MAJCOM may only perform 
one drug testing role in an inspection. MAJCOMs’ responsibilities for all units 
designated under their Base Area Code (BAC) include:

a. Review monthly reports provided by the DoD Internet Forensic Toxicology Drug 
Testing Laboratory (iFTDTL) Web Drug Testing Portal (WebDTP) and forward 
reports to all designated units’ DTCs/ADTCs;

b. Provide designated units with urinalysis test results within two days from appearing 
in the iFTDTL WebDTP;

nclude:

a. Review monthly reports provided by the DoD Internet Forensic Toxicology Drug 
Testing Laboratory (iFTDTL) Web Drug Testing Portal (WebDTP) and forward 
reports to all designated units’ DTCs/ADTCs;

b. Provide designated units with urinalysis test results within two days from appearing 
in the iFTDTL WebDTP;

c. Report positive results to designated units and ensure units have notified their 
CO/OIC and local CGIS office within 24 hours from notification of a positive 
urinalysis test;

d. Assist designated units with rosters and review units’ urinalysis access documentation 
for correctness before submitting it to the PM;

e. Monitor and track designated unit testing quotas through iFTDTL WebDTP to ensure 
units are meeting quarterly and yearly testing requirements; 
6-3

f. Notify the PM of the units that do not meet the quarter or annual quotas;

g. Review allocations showing all designated units to ensure units are accurately 
represented;

h. Submit an updated unit listing to the PM each August;

i. Act as the Point of Contact (POC) for all questions by unit DTC/ADTCs regarding 
the Drug Testing program;

j. Monitor the CGBI Drug Testing Report to ensure that all unit personnel (100 percent 
assigned) are urinalysis tested at least once during the FY, in accordance with 
Paragraph 6.D.1.d., Random Selection Testing Requirements, of this Instruction; and,

k. Maintain records of pertinent correspondence of WebDTP access documents for their 
unit, including but not limited to designation memos, training documentation, positive 
drug testing results, a roster of each coordinator attached to each unit designated, and 
other documentation necessary to record a unit’s program. Records must be 
maintained in accordance with the procedures on the CG-PSC-PSD-FS CG portal site 
at https://cg.portal.uscg.mil/units/psc/FS/default.aspx. 
Drug Testing Coordinators (DTC). The DTC replaces the Urinalysis Coordinator (UC) 
within this program and is responsible for the Unit’s Drug Testing Program maintenance 
and administration. DTC duties must reside with the unit’s Executive Officer (XO), 
Deputy, or Executive Petty Officer (XPO). COs/OICs must designate, in writing, the 
DTC and ADTC in the Command Collateral Duty List. The designation of DTC is non-
delegable. However, units with designated Commanding Officers of Military Personnel 
(e.g., Area Personnel Division Chiefs, Sector Logistics Department Heads, Base 
Personnel Support Department Heads) may assign DTC duties to these officers, provided 
they are Lieutenant Commander (O4) or above. One DTC will be identified per unit and 
will be responsible and accountable for the oversight and management of the Unit Drug 
Testing Program. If the DTC is designated for multiple drug testing roles, the DTC may 
only perform one drug testing role during an inspection. The DTC’s responsibilities are:

a. Ultimately responsible for the Unit Drug Testing Program maintenance and 
administration, including training of DTOs, SCs, SVs, and ADTCs, and shipping of 
uncompromised specimens to Tripler Army Medical Center (TAMC) FTDTL;

b. Ensures processing procedures in the contracted laboratory’s procedures and on the 
CG-PSC-PSD-FS CG portal page at 
https://cg.portal.uscg.mil/units/psc/FS/default.aspx are followed;

c. Supervises the specimen collection process; 
6-4

d. Reviews drug testing reports;

e. Makes testing register entries in WebDTP, if the DTC is coordinating the test;

program. Records must be maintained in accordance with the procedures on the CG-
PSC-PSD-FS CG portal site at https://cg.portal.uscg.mil/units/psc/FS/default.aspx;

i. Identify all members that are exempt in accordance with Paragraph 6.D.1.e, 
Exceptions to Sample Provision, of this Instruction. ADTC must ensure that excusals 
are documented in accordance with the procedures on the CG-PSC-PSD-FS CG 
portal site at https://cg.portal.uscg.mil/units/psc/FS/default.aspx; and, 
j. 
 Second Verifier (SV). The SV is designated in writing by the command and is a Coast 
Guard member whose main responsibility is to conduct a secondary review of the 
specimens collected during an inspection. This includes, but is not limited to checking the 
bottle for tightness, ensuring bottles are completely dry before packaging, ensuring 
samples are packaged correctly, and providing assistance to the DTC/ADTC as needed. 
The SV’s duties and responsibilities are outlined in the urinalysis checklist that is located 
on the CG-PSC-PD-FS CG portal site at: 
https://cg.portal.uscg.mil/units/psc/FS/default.aspx. The requirements for an SV are:

a. SVs must be at pay grade E-5 or higher or a member of the Command Cadre;

b. Units must use an SV to assist in drug testing procedures;

c. Units may designate as many SVs as the unit needs; and,

d. If an SV is designated for multiple drug testing roles, the SV may only perform one 
drug testing role during an inspection. 
Drug Testing Observer (DTO). A DTO replaces the former Urinalysis Observer (UO). 
The DTO is designated in writing by the command and is a Coast Guard member whose 
6-6

main responsibility is to escort members selected for testing into the design ated area for 
the purpose of observing their specimen collection.

(2) Observe specimen collection;

(3) Testify at an administrative hearing or at a court-martial, as needed;

(4) Follow all other procedures in the contracted laboratory's procedures 
and on the CG-PSC-PSD-FS CG portal site at: 
https :/ /cg. po1ial. uscg.mil/units/psc/FS/default.aspx.

8. Specimen Collectors (SC). The SC must be designated in writing by the CO/OIC in the 
Command Collateral Duty List and is a Coast Guard member whose primary 
responsibility is to collect HST and OFT samples. The SC must be of the same sex as the 
member being tested while the sample is collected. In circumstances where a SC with the 
same sex is not available, the DTC/ADTC must coordinate testing with another unit.

a. The main duties of SCs are to:

(1) Obtain and process hair and oral fluid samples from a service member in 
accordance with the contracted testing facility's procedures; and,

(2) Testify at an administrative hearing or at a court-martial, as needed.

9. Testing ofDTCs, ADTCs, SVs, SCs, and DTOs. All personnel involved in collection and 
shipment of samples will not place and/or ship their own samples in the same batches 
6-7

they are responsible for collecting or shipping. The chain of custody of samples must be 
maintained, documented, and confirmed by the DTC.

a. An ADTC, DTC, SV, SCs, and DTOs (“Inspection Team”) from another unit must be 
used when the command requires a unit sweep, as defined in Paragraph 6.D.1.b, 
Application, and the unit’s inspection team is being tested as part of that sweep. An 
ADTC or DTC from another unit must be used when the command requires testing of 
its own DTCs and ADTCs. The command must coordinate with the other unit to 
provide random drug testing for unit coordinators.

b. Commands may temporarily defer the requirements in Paragraph 6.C.9.a of this 
Instruction to the unit’s ADTC or DTC if remote or isolated assignments make 
coordination unreasonably difficult or not possible. The unit must have at least one 
DTC, ADTC, and SV, and several DTOs and SCs. The ADTC will process the 
sample without the DTC’s presence or influence in circumstances where the DTC is 
selected for random drug testing. The same applies to the DTC if an ADTC is 
randomly selected. Examples of influence include, but are not limited to DTCs 
removing themselves from “to-be-tested” lists, and watching labeling, taping, or 
shipping their own sample(s). The selected DTC, or ADTC, will have their sample 
collected, labeled, and shipped by an authorized DTC or ADTC like any other 
member being subjected to testing. If both the DTC and the ADTC are selected for 
testing, randomization to designate a coordinator must be established. The DTC or 
ADTC that is not randomly selected to provide a sample must be designated to 
coordinate testing in accordance with CG PSC PSD FS's procedures found on their 
portal site at  https://cg.portal.uscg.mil/units/psc/FS/default.aspx.

c. Access to the drug test ledger and specimen collection materials must be limited to 
the CO/OIC, XO/XPO, DTC, and ADTCs, when such access is necessary. 
DoD Commands. Coast Guard members assigned to DoD commands are subject to the 
respective Service’s drug administration and testing program. DoD members serving in 
the Coast Guard are subject to Coast Guard’s drug administration and testing program, 
unless otherwise dictated by the individual terms of the assignment. If a Coast Guard 
member’s urinalysis, blood test, HST, OFT, or other approved additional drug test reports 
a positive result, the DoD command generally immediately notifies the member’s 
cognizant Coast Guard PM, who follows the procedures outlined in Chapter 5, Drug 
Incidents, of this Instruction.

D. Circumstances for Conducting Drug Testing. The procedures described below apply to the 
command drug testing programs and are not binding on investigations conducted by CGIS or 
other military criminal investigative organizations. 
6-8

Administrative Inspections.

a. Designed Purpose. Administrative inspections are designed to determine and 
maintain the unit’s security, military readiness, and good order and discipline in 
accordance with the Military Rules of Evidence (M.R.E) 313(b). M.R.E. 313(b) states 
that an inspection is an examination of the whole or part of a unit, organization, 
installation, vessel, aircraft, or vehicle, including an examination conducted at 
entrance and exit points, conducted as an incident of command the primary purpose 
of which is to determine and to ensure the security, military fitness, or good order and 
discipline of the unit, organization, installation, vessel, aircraft, or vehicle. An 
inspection may include, but is not limited to, an examination to determine and to 
ensure that any or all of the following requirements are met: that the command is 
appropriately equipped, functioning properly, maintaining proper standards of 
readiness, sea or airworthiness, sanitation and cleanliness; and that personnel are 
present, fit, and ready for duty. Such an inspection is allowed even if it was directed 
immediately following the report of a specific offense in the unit and was not 
previously scheduled. An order to produce body fluids, such as urine, is permissible 
in accordance with this rule.

b. Application. M.R.E 313(b) allows drug testing for administrative inspections using 
urinalysis or additional drug testing when they are primarily conducted for the 
designed purposes and are based on a random selection of members or units (“unit 
sweeps”) to be inspected. 
Members of the Reserve component serving in any duty status are subject to the 
same random selection or unit sweeps for an administrative inspection as their 
active duty counterparts; 
An inspection that meets these requirements does not require a member’s 
consent, probable cause, or search authorization, to collect a specimen; 
Substance testing that targets certain members or is conducted for the primary 
purpose of obtaining evidence for use in a trial by court-martial or in other 
disciplinary proceedings is not permitted for administrative inspections and 
requires probable cause, search and seizure authorization.

c. Scope. The inspection’s scope may include military personnel of the whole unit, 
installation, vessel, or aircraft, or only part of the unit (e.g., duty section, division, 
building occupants, etc.), provided that part of the unit includes enough members for 
the inspection to not be viewed as targeting a particular individual or small group of 
individuals. An administrative inspection may be conducted with or without a notice 
to the inspected, but notice is not required or recommended.  
6-9

the inspection to not be viewed as targeting a particular individual or small group of

6-9

d. Random Selection Testing Requirements. 
Random selection is a statistical specimen selection method in which all 
possible specimens have equal probability of selection. The purpose is to 
provide all military members with the same participation probability in the 
controlled and prohibited substance testing program. Random sampling 
allocations must be based on the number of personnel assigned to the unit. The 
DoD iFTDTL software may be used in the selection for random urinalysis 
testing. 
All active duty and reserve service members in a drilling status must be 
randomly urinalysis tested, at a minimum, once annually. Commands must 
conduct an end of FY unit or sub-unit sweep of any individuals not tested 
during the FY to ensure all service members assigned to the unit have been 
randomly tested at least once annually. All (100 percent assigned) personnel 
assigned to a unit at the end of the FY must have been successfully tested at 
least once during the FY. 
Units must test 25 percent of assigned personnel quarterly. It is recommended 
that units test at least 10 percent of assigned personnel monthly until the 
quarterly requirement is met. 
DTCs or ADTCs must contact their servicing legal office and CG-PSC-PSD-FS 
for guidance if members are not able to provide a sample for random urinalysis 
testing and the members do not meet the exceptions in Paragraph 6.D.1.e., 
Exceptions to Sample Provision, of this Instruction.

e. Exceptions to Sample Provision. All military personnel randomly selected for 
urinalysis and/or additional drug testing must submit a sample unless they are on: 
Previously approved leave, including non-chargeable rest and recuperation 
leave. Leave requested after notification of a urinalysis or additional drug 
testing must be denied; 
Temporarily Assigned Duty (TAD/TDY)/Permissive Orders; 
Pre-approved administrative absence including elective medical care; 
Rotational duty status and considered off-duty; 
Post deployment mobilization respite absence; or, 
Medical provider-directed sick in quarters/sick at home duty status.

f. Telework and Remote Work. Military personnel teleworking, using remote site work 
6-10

locations, or assigned to operating locations away from their parent unit in 
accordance with References (q) and (r) are NOT excused from unit-directed 
urinalysis or additional drug testing. The DTC/ADTC for a member’s assigned unit 
must coordinate with the DTC/ADTC that is geographically closest to the member’s 
official worksite to perform the urinalysis or additional drug test prior to a member’s 
selection for urinalysis or drug testing. Teleworking or remote site working members 
must report to the geographic unit that has been designated to perform their urinalysis 
or additional drug testing. Units must consult with CG-PCS-PSD-FS for direction in 
situations where coordination between units is not possible (such as geographically 
disbursed remote work locations).

g. Refusal to Provide a Specimen. The DTC must notify the appropriate command 
authority if a member refuses to provide a specimen,. The command representative 
should reiterate the order to provide a specimen to the member in front of a witness. 
In doing so, the command representative should ensure the member understands that 
failure to provide a sample would constitute the violation of a lawful order and could 
subject the member to disciplinary action under the UCMJ, as well as administrative 
action up to and including administrative separation. Further refusal constitutes a DI 
and a violation of a direct order that will subject the member to disciplinary action 
under the UCMJ. Possible actions include court-martial proceedings and processing 
for separation. Commands should consider giving a member a witnessed written order 
that requires the member to not shave their head or body prior to a sample being taken 
if the possibility of hair specimen sampling exists. 
Consent-Based Testing. Substance testing may be conducted at any time and for any 
reason if the member voluntarily consents to provide a specimen. When requesting 
consent, the member must be advised that he or she may decline to submit a specimen; 
limit consent to a certain time, place, or specimen to be collected; and that they may 
revoke their consent at any time. Consent must be obtained in writing. Consent is not 
voluntary if a member submits to the color of authority of law enforcement personnel or 
command (the appearance of authority or legal right to take action against a member), or 
provides a specimen after the command orders a drug test should the member refuse to 
consent. 
Search and Seizure Authorization. A military member may be ordered to submit to a 
substance test if there is probable cause to believe the member used a controlled or 
prohibited substance and a substance test will produce evidence of such use. Commands 
must obtain a search and seizure authorization before collecting a specimen unless there 
are exigent circumstances creating a reasonable belief that the evidence of substance use 
will be destroyed upon delay. Commands must consult with their servicing legal office 
prior to executing a drug test under this subparagraph. 
6-11

Valid-Medical Purpose Testing.

a. Occurrence. Qualified medical personnel may direct drug testing for diagnostic 
(mild, moderate, or severe) or treatment purposes during a medical examination 
(emergency care, periodic physical examinations, and such other medical 
examinations as are necessary for diagnostic or treatment purposes). Follow-up tests 
to confirm a diagnosis or to monitor a condition are included in this category. Such 
testing cannot exceed what is necessary for the medical purpose. Substance testing 
directed by the member’s command (such as a fitness-for-duty test) does not fall 
within this subsection. This testing may be voluntary or involuntary.

testing cannot exceed what is necessary for the medical purpose. Substance testing 
directed by the member’s command (such as a fitness-for-duty test) does not fall 
within this subsection. This testing may be voluntary or involuntary.

b. Limits on Use of Patient Records. A positive biometric test (e.g. urinalysis, blood, 
OFT), or other record generated in the course of a substance abuse treatment program 
may not be disclosed or used in any administrative or criminal proceeding, consistent 
with 42 C.F.R. § 2.12 and § 2.13. These protections begin at the time the member 
self-refers or is command directed to a substance abuse treatment program. This 
provision does not prohibit commands from documenting a Drug Incident if, in the 
course of follow-up or evaluation testing, the member tests positive for a banned 
substance. Information obtained as part of follow-up or evaluation testing may be 
used as the basis for a DI, even though the testing is consistent with a medical 
treatment plan. 
Fitness-for-Duty Test. Fitness-for-Duty Test includes Evaluation Testing, Command-
Directed Testing, and a Reportable Mishap Testing. These tests are used to determine a 
member’s fitness for duty or need for counseling, rehabilitation, or medical treatment.

a. Command-Directed Testing. CO/OICs may order command-directed testing of Coast 
Guard members when the member’s uncharacteristic or bizarre behavior (such as 
belligerence, mood lability, or impaired judgment), breaches of discipline, and other 
similar acts give rise to a reasonable suspicion of controlled or prohibited substance 
use. 
A reasonable suspicion is a less demanding standard than probable cause and is 
defined as something more than a vague suspicion or hunch. Reasonable 
suspicion requires a minimal level of justification and can arise from 
information less reliable than that required to show probable cause. Unusual 
behavior or conduct that a CO/OIC should consider includes but is not limited 
to: unauthorized absences; safety violations; disobeying direct orders; 
apprehension or investigation for drug offenses or intoxicated driving; 
involvement in crimes of violence; reckless equipment operation; involvement 
in a serious incident or accident in which unusually careless acts were 
performed; or other incidents involving serious discipline breaches. While an 
6-12

instance of unusual behavior or conduct alone is not necessarily sufficient to 
constitute a reasonable suspicion, such behavior or conduct must be considered 
within the totality of the circumstances. 
The CO/OIC is authorized to order this test and that authority may be delegated 
to an XO/XPO or a Command-Duty officer. Fitness-for-duty substance test 
results may be used to refer a member for administrative separation action. 
Results may also be used for impeachment or rebuttal in court-martial 
proceedings, subject to M.R.E. However, commands may not use a Fitness-for-
Duty drug test to take punitive action, including non-judicial punishment, 
against a member under the UCMJ.

Results may also be used for impeachment or rebuttal in court-martial

against a member under the UCMJ.

b. Evaluation Testing. CO/OICs may order evaluation testing of Coast Guard members 
when a member's urine specimen, blood, hair, or oral fluid is reported as confirmed 
positive for the presence of controlled or prohibited substance(s) or when it is 
prescribed in a member’s medical treatment plan. Re-testing of negative samples is 
not authorized, unless the selection of the samples is done on a random basis as 
discussed at Paragraph 6.D.1.d, Random Selection Testing Requirements. 
The CO/OIC must adhere to the testing frequency and requirements outlined in 
a member’s medical treatment plan.The medical treatment plan must be 
prescribed by a CG medical officer and issued in accordance with Reference 
(a). 
When a medical treatment plan is not prescribed to a member, a CO/OIC may 
order evaluation testing to be conducted for a two- to six-month duration. If 
urinalysis is the selected testing method, no more than 16 specimens may be 
taken. For example, two specimens per week for eight weeks; one specimen per 
week for 16 weeks; or up to 16 total specimens over a six-month period 
collected at irregular intervals. If HST is selected, no more than three testing 
submissions may be taken over a six-month period. 
Except under the conditions outlined in Paragraph 6.D.4.b, Limits on Use of 
Patient Records, of this Instruction, the original positive drug test result may 
still be used as a basis for disciplinary action under the UCMJ, administrative 
separation, and discharge characterization, if supported by the justification for 
the original test, such as, probable cause, administrative inspection, consent, or 
fitness-for-duty test. See Paragraph 6.D, Circumstances for Conducting Drug 
Testing, of this Instruction. 
Depending on the basis for Command-Ordered Testing, positive results from 
that test may be used for any purpose, including disciplinary action under the 
UCMJ, administrative separation, and discharge characterization in 
6-13

administrative separation proceedings. See Reference (b), Chapter 1. 
Commands must document Command-Ordered evaluation testing in 
Administrative Remarks, Form CG-3307 (P&D – 06).

administrative separation proceedings. See Reference (b), Chapter 1. 
Commands must document Command-Ordered evaluation testing in 
Administrative Remarks, Form CG-3307 (P&D – 06).

c. Reportable Mishap Testing. A reportable mishap, as defined in Reference (e) is any 
unplanned, unexpected, or unintentional event causing injury, occupational illness, 
death, or material loss or damage. Specimens of urine, blood, hair, oral fluid, and/or 
breath must be collected from all members involved in a Class A or B mishap in 
accordance with Reference (e). Collection of the specimen following Class C, D, and 
E mishaps is at command discretion with certain exceptions listed in section E.5(d)(2) 
of Chapter 3 of Reference (e). After-mishap testing results may be used in 
administrative separation proceedings following Commandant (CG-11) and (CG-094) 
approval for release of information, in accordance with section G.2 of Chapter 3 of 
Reference (e). 
Drug Testing Conducted in Conjunction with Training. All accessions and Coast Guard 
members ordered to attend Coast Guard training are subject to a medical-purpose 
examination that may include urinalysis, blood test, HST, OFT, or approved additional 
drug tests. They are also subject to random substance testing through administrative 
inspections while assigned to a Coast Guard training command. Training commands must 
conduct testing as follows:

a. Initial Enlistment/Commission. 
Each person applying for an initial enlistment to include recruits or appointment 
as cadets, officer candidates (including Reserve Officer Indoctrination 
candidates), and direct commission officers must typically be counseled on the 
policies in this Instruction no less than 30 days prior to their scheduled 
departure date to a training facility. The recruiting office will counsel the 
individual at the soonest possible date where counseling cannot occur at least 30 
days prior to the scheduled departure date. Counseling must be documented in 
an Administrative Remarks, Form CG-3307 (P&D – 06), and submitted to 
Coast Guard Recruiting Command (CGRC). Counseling is strictly an awareness 
mechanism to ensure the individual is aware of the provisions that they are 
subject to under this Instruction. 
Each person applying for an initial enlistment to include recruits or appointment 
as cadets, officer candidates (including Reserve Officer Indoctrination 
candidates), and direct commission officers must be tested within 72 hours after 
reporting to a training facility. Individuals who have been directed to 
“quarantine” or “isolate,” as those terms are defined below, by a Coast Guard 
health care provider are exempt from this policy during the duration of their 
6-14

quarantine or isolation. Individuals must, however, be tested within 72-hours 
after being released from “quarantine” or “isolation” and reporting to a training 
facility. Instances where an individual receives a positive test for Delta-8 THC, 
as defined in Paragraph 7.A.2, Marijuana and Tetrahydrocannabinol (THC), 
following an Initial Enlistment/Commission drug test will result in a No-Drug 
Incident in accordance with Paragraph 5.H.5, “No-Drug Incident” Finding.

facility. Instances where an individual receives a positive test for Delta-8 THC,

(a) Quarantine means the separation of an individual or group of individuals 
who has been exposed to a communicable disease, but is not yet showing 
the symptoms of illness, from others who have not been so exposed, in 
such manner and place to prevent the possible spread of the communicable 
disease.

(b) Isolation, in opposition to quarantine, means the separation of an individual 
or group of individuals infected and/or suspected to be infected with a 
communicable disease from those who are healthy in such a place and 
manner to prevent the spread of the communicable disease. 
b. “A” School Students. All training centers conducting “A” schools must test “A” 
school students within 96 hours of the students’ reporting to a training facility. Coast 
Guard members who have been directed to “quarantine” or “isolate,” as those terms 
are defined in Paragraph 6.D.6.a.(2) (a) and (b), by a Coast Guard health care 
provider are exempt from this requirement during the duration of their quarantine or 
isolation. “A” school students must be tested within 96 hours after being released 
from “quarantine” or “isolation” and reporting to a training facility.

c. Duty Under Instruction (DUINS). DUINS students are subject to the same random 
testing as all military members. Resident school students attending an institution 
sponsored by another branch of the Armed Forces are subject to the standards 
required by that institution within the command to which they are attached as well as 
the sponsoring Coast Guard PM. All other students are subject to the requirements set 
forth by the PM, MAJCOM, and DTC within the jurisdiction in which the member 
resides or attends school. 
Prior Service Members upon Return to Active Duty. All military members who report to 
active duty, either active duty or Selected Reserve, who have a break in service greater 
than six months, may be tested by consent, at a medical-purpose examination, for fitness-
for-duty, and/or during an administrative inspection. 
6-15

E. Sample Adulteration, Substitution, Dilution and Refusal to Follow Contracted 
Guidelines 
Actions upon Suspicion of Tampering. DTOs who suspect that a member tampered with 
their specimen (e.g., the observer saw them put something in the specimen), must report 
it to the DTC/ADTC conducting the testing, who will then secure the specimen and 
notify the CO/OIC or appropriate chain-of-command. The CO/OIC or designee will 
direct the member to provide another specimen under the "Command-directed" premise 
code. Both the original and new specimen will be sent to the screening laboratory for 
testing. 
Drug Screening Laboratory. The drug screening laboratory is able to detect adulterated or 
substituted samples. The laboratory will notify the command when a sample is suspected 
to contain adulterants. The command may request that the laboratory send the specimen 
to another laboratory for further adulterant testing and forward the results to the 
requesting command. 
UCMJ Implications for Inspection Teams and tested military members. Any Inspection 
Team member (DTC, ADTC, DTO, SV, or SC) and tested military members who 
knowingly fail or refuse to follow this Instruction or the procedures of a contracted 
testing facility may be subject to discipline under UCMJ. A member who knowingly 
tampers with their or anyone else’s specimen, refuses to provide a specimen, and/or 
causes, entices, or attempts to influence an Inspection Team member to refrain from 
observing the collection of the specimen or performance of any other related duties may 
be subject to discipline under the UCMJ.

source: CG Military Substance Abuse and Behavioral Addiction Program (COMDTINST 1000.10B)

ember who knowingly 
tampers with their or anyone else’s specimen, refuses to provide a specimen, and/or 
causes, entices, or attempts to influence an Inspection Team member to refrain from 
observing the collection of the specimen or performance of any other related duties may 
be subject to discipline under the UCMJ.

F. Authorized Drug Testing.

1. Urinalysis. Urinalysis testing is the procedure employed to obtain urine samples under 
controlled conditions, maintain a chain of custody on each sample, and scientifically 
analyze the samples to detect the presence of controlled/prohibited substances or their 
metabolytes. Urinalysis testing is the Coast Guard’s primary form of drug testing. 
Authorized additional forms of drug testing covered in this Chapter may supplement the 
urinalysis testing program but must not replace the urinalysis testing requirements in 
Paragraph 6.D.1.d. Random Selection Testing Requirements. The authorized additional 
forms of drug testing covered in Paragraph 6.F, Authorized Drug Testing, in this 
Instruction may require unit funding.

a. Frequency. All active duty and reserve service members in a drilling status must be 
randomly urinalysis tested, at a minimum, once annually in accordance with 
Paragraph 6.D.1.d. Random Selection Testing Requirements.

b. Urinalysis Procedures. Commands must use the procedures on CG-PSD-PSC-FS’s 
6-16

CG portal site at https://cg.portal.uscg.mil/units/psc/FS/default.aspx to conduct 
urinalysis testing, which includes best practices and guidance for use by all members 
performing and participating in this program.

2. Hair Specimen Testing (HST). HST is an authorized additional form of drug testing. HST 
is the procedure used to obtain a hair sample under controlled conditions, maintain a 
chain of custody on each sample, and scientifically analyze the samples to detect the 
presence of controlled substances and prohibited non-controlled substances or their 
metabolytes. HST is a valuable tool to complement urinalysis in detection and deterrence 
of drug use. This provision does not alter the existing CGIS authority to use HST to 
conduct drug-related investigations based on consent or probable cause. The HST 
procedures described below apply to Command-directed drug testing for administrative 
inspections.

a. Collecting Hair Sample Specimens. The DTC, ADTC, or SC will document and 
process the collection of each sample in accordance with the procedures of the 
contracted testing facility.

b. HST Results. Positive HST results may constitute a DI and must be reported to the 
MAJCOM and the local CGIS office in accordance with Paragraph 6.C.2, 
Commanding Officers (COs) and Officers-in-Charge (OICs). This requirement does 
not apply to HSTs conducted during the course of a substance abuse treatment 
program, as indicated in Paragraph 6.D.4.b, Limits on Use of Patient Records, of this 
Instruction.

3. Oral Fluid Test (OFT). OFT is an authorized additional form of drug testing. OFT is the 
procedure employed to obtain oral fluid, or saliva samples under controlled conditions, 
maintaining a chain of custody on each sample, and scientifically analyze the samples to 
detect the presence of controlled substances and prohibited non-controlled substances or 
their metabolytes.

a. Collecting Fluid Sample Specimens. The DTC, ADTC or SC will document and 
process the collection of each sample in accordance with the procedures of the 
contracted testing facility.

b. OFT Results. Positive OFT results may constitute a DI and must be reported to the

4. Blood Test. Blood testing is an authorized additional form of drug testing. Blood testing 
is the procedure employed to obtain blood samples under controlled conditions, 
maintaining a chain of custody on each sample, and scientifically analyze the samples to 
detect the presence of controlled substances and prohibited non-controlled substances or 
their metabolytes.

a. Collecting Blood Sample Specimens. Only a qualified medical person may collect a 
blood sample specimen. Blood tests must be conducted in accordance with the 
procedures of the contracted testing facility.

b. Blood Test Results. Positive blood test results may constitute a DI and must be 
reported to the MAJCOM and the local CGIS office in accordance with Paragraph 
6.C.2, Commanding Officers (COs) and Officers-in-Charge (OICs). This requirement 
does not apply to blood tests conducted during the course of a substance abuse 
treatment program, as indicated in Paragraph 6.D.4.b, Limits on Use of Patient 
Records, of this Instruction.

5. Steroid and Controlled Performance Enhancing Drug Testing. Steriod and controlled 
performance enhancing drug testing is an authorized additional form of drug testing. 
Steroid and controlled performance enhancing drug testing is the procedure employed to 
test for evidence of anabolic steroids, anabolic agents, and any drug, selective androgen 
receptor modulator (SARM), growth hormone, blood-boosting agent, side effect 
modulator, or masking agent intended to enhance or conceal the use of anabolic steroids 
listed in 21 U.S.C. § 802. Anabolic steroids are any drug or hormonal substance, 
chemically and pharmacologically related to testosterone (other than estrogen, progestin 
and corticosteroid) that promotes muscle growth and includes any salt, ester or ether of 
such a drug or substance described or listed in the Designer Anabolic Steroid Control Act 
of 2014 and subsequent amendments or 21 U.S.C. § 802, if that salt, ester or ether 
promotes muscle growth. Testing for steroids and controlled performance enhancing 
drugs is typically conducted through urinalysis testing. Other methods of authorized 
additional drug testing described in Paragraph. 6.F., Authorized Drug Testing, of this 
Instruction are acceptable for steroid and controlled performance enhancing drug testing.

a. Collecting Steroid and Controlled Performance Enhancing Drug Specimens. The 
DTC, ADTC, DTO, or SC will document and process the collection of each sample in 
accordance with the procedures of the contracted testing facility.

section: Chapter 7 -- Prohibition on Hemp and Marijuana Establishments
chapter_title: Prohibition on Hemp and Marijuana Establishments
heading_path: Prohibition on Hemp and Marijuana Establishments
text:
A. Hemp Policy. 
Objective. Wrongful use of controlled substances, including marijuana, poses a 
significant risk to Coast Guard personnel and to unit readiness, and negatively impacts 
mission execution. The use, possession, manufacture, distribution, or export/import of 
marijuana is a violation of federal law and the UCMJ. 
Marijuana and Tetrahydrocannabinol (THC). Marijuana is defined as all parts of the plant 
cannabis sativa L., the seeds thereof, and every derivative thereof. See 21 U.S.C § 
802(16A). THC, including but not limited to Delta-8, Delta-9, and Delta-10 THC, are the 
main psychoactive chemical compound in marijuana, which elicits the mind-altering and 
euphoric sensations. 
Hemp. Hemp is defined by 7 U.S.C. § 1639o and means the plant cannabis sativa L., and 
any part of that plant, including the seeds thereof and all derivatives, extracts, 
cannabinoids, isomers, acids, salts, and salts of isomers, whether growing or not, with a 
Delta-9 THC concentration of not more than 0.3 percent on a dry weight basis. 
Cannabidiol (CBD). Similar to hemp, hemp-derived CBD contains up to 0.3 percent of 
THC. Hemp containing more than 0.3 percent of THC and non-hemp-derived CBD 
(including cannabis-derived CBD and synthetic CBD) fall under the definition of 
marijuana above and are considered controlled substances under the Controlled 
Substance Act (CSA) and Article 112a, UCMJ.  
Federal Regulations. Hemp and hemp-derived CBD products are commercially sold as 
oils, vape oils, capsules, gum, concentrates, and edibles (protein bars, spreads, sauces, 
juices, cereal, snacks, and candies).

a. Lack of Proper Regulations. While the Food and Drug Administration (FDA) 
regulates some of these products (hulled hemp seeds, hemp seed protein powder, and 
hemp seed oil), many other unregulated products may pose some risk of being 
contaminated with THC above 0.3 percent, synthetic CBD, THC, or counterfeit 
versions of the same. These products could omit any reference to THC on the product 
label, list inaccurate ingredients, or both. In addition, the lack of proper regulations 
creates the following risks for the Coast Guard members: 
Accumulation in Body. THC, including THC contained in ingredients on the 
FDA’s Generally-Recognized-As-Safe (GRAS) List, can accumulate in a 
human body to detectable levels. Consequently, Coast Guard members cannot 
rely on packaging or labeling of hemp and hemp-derived CBD products in 
determining whether the product contains THC concentrations above 0.3 
7-1

percent or is otherwise safe to consume.  
Contamination by other substances. Without proper FDA or U.S. Department 
of Agriculture (USDA) regulation, it is possible hemp or hemp-derived CBD 
products could contain ingredients hazardous to Coast Guard members’ health. 
Without proper regulation a member has little assurance that the products are 
safe for consumption or use.

source: CG Military Substance Abuse and Behavioral Addiction Program (COMDTINST 1000.10B)
section: Chapter 7 -- Prohibition on Hemp and Marijuana Establishments
chapter_title: Prohibition on Hemp and Marijuana Establishments
heading_path: Prohibition on Hemp and Marijuana Establishments
text:
by other substances. Without proper FDA or U.S. Department 
of Agriculture (USDA) regulation, it is possible hemp or hemp-derived CBD 
products could contain ingredients hazardous to Coast Guard members’ health. 
Without proper regulation a member has little assurance that the products are 
safe for consumption or use.

b. Interference with the Coast Guard Drug Inspection and Testing Program. The use of 
these hemp and hemp-derived CBD products, both regulated and unregulated, may 
interfere with the effective administration of Coast Guard drug-testing program and 
the safety and operational readiness of Coast Guard members. The use of certain 
hemp and hemp-derived CBD products could result in a reporting of unlawful THC 
levels in Coast Guard members, even if a member had used only legal hemp and 
hemp-derived CBD products. The use of these products may undermine the Coast 
Guard’s ability to effectively identify use of controlled substances and to ensure 
safety and operational readiness of its personnel.

c. Coast Guard Hemp Policy. This Instruction establishes the following policy to 
protect the reliability and integrity of the Coast Guard drug-testing program and to 
ensure safety and operational readiness of Coast Guard military personnel. 
All Coast Guard members are prohibited from using any products 
containing hemp as defined by 7 U.S.C. § 1639o, including its extracts and 
derivatives (such as hemp-derived CBD, hulled hemp seeds, hemp seed protein 
powder, and hemp seed oil), regardless of the product’s THC concentration, 
claimed or actual, and regardless of whether the product may be lawfully 
purchased, sold, and/or used under Federal or state law applicable to civilians. 
This is a lawful punitive order and failure by Coast Guard members to comply 
with these provisions is a violation of the Article 92, UCMJ, Failure to Obey a 
Lawful General Order; and any other UCMJ Article that may apply. Violations 
may result in administrative or criminal action(s). 
For the purposes of this policy, “use” means to inject, ingest, inhale, vape, 
apply to skin or hair, or otherwise introduce into the human body. “Use” 
includes use of hemp or hemp-derived CBD products designed to penetrate, be 
absorbed or applied into or onto the skin layer, including but not limited to 
transdermal patches, and topical products (such as soaps, lotions, conditioners, 
shampoos, lip balms, and other topical products). 
This prohibition does not apply to the ingestion, consumption, or use of 
cannabinoid formulations approved as drugs by the FDA, for which the service 
member has a valid prescription, such as Dronabinol (Marinol®, Syndros®) and 
7-2

h as soaps, lotions, conditioners, 
shampoos, lip balms, and other topical products). 
This prohibition does not apply to the ingestion, consumption, or use of 
cannabinoid formulations approved as drugs by the FDA, for which the service 
member has a valid prescription, such as Dronabinol (Marinol®, Syndros®) and 
7-2

cannabidiol (Epidiolex®). A prescription is valid if issued by a Coast Guard 
clinic, a DoD medical facility, or a civilian medical service provider approved 
by the Coast Guard or DoD. 
This prohibition does not apply to use pursuant to legitimate law-enforcement 
activities, by authorized personnel in the performance of medical duties, or 
without knowledge that the product was made or derived from hemp, including 
hemp-derived CBD, where that lack of knowledge is reasonable. This 
prohibition also does not apply to the use of durable goods containing hemp, 
such as rope or clothing. 
All other substances that do not meet the definition of hemp above, such as 
hemp containing more than 0.3 percent of THC, and non-hemp-derived CBD, 
(including cannabis-derived CBD, and synthetic and counterfeit CBD and THC) 
are considered controlled substances under the CSA and UCMJ, and are 
prohibited from being wrongfully used, possessed, manufactured, distributed, or 
imported as stated in this policy or pursuant to Article 112a, UCMJ.

(including cannabis-derived CBD, and synthetic and counterfeit CBD and THC) 
are considered controlled substances under the CSA and UCMJ, and are 
prohibited from being wrongfully used, possessed, manufactured, distributed, or

B. Marijuana Establishments. 
Prohibition of Marijuana Use. Despite legalization in several states, the use, possession, 
and distribution of marijuana remains prohibited under federal law and the UCMJ. 
Association with Marijuana Businesses. Under Article 134, UCMJ, it is unlawful to 
knowingly associate with or perform the duties of an owner, operator, vendor, or direct 
investor for a Marijuana Business, even where permitted by state or local laws. 
Participation in or close association (such as businesses owned/operated by 
spouse/significant other) with marijuana growth or distribution commercial enterprises, 
including assisting or encouraging these commercial enterprises, is also a violation of the 
UCMJ.

a. Direct Investor. For the purposes of this prohibition, a direct investor means a Coast 
Guard member, who personally (without an agent or a third-party) and knowingly 
transfer(s) money or goods to an individual or a Marijuana Business, to acquire any 
interest in, or to establish or operate, a Marijuana Business (or its physical assets) 
with a purpose of producing income or capital gains. 
Exceptions. The direct investor definition does not include and does not apply 
to:

(a) The purchase or ownership of mutual funds or securities on the open 
market, which may include an enterprise as defined above, for the sole 
purpose of investment and without the intention of controlling or 
7-3

participating in the control of the issuer, or election of one or more of its 
directors, or of assisting another individual or an enterprise in doing so.

(b) Businesses, whose primary purpose is not growth, manufacture, sale, 
import/export, or distribution of marijuana or THC products and other 
related items, such as bookstores, cafes, bakeries, clothing stores, gas 
stations, etc.

text:
re of its 
directors, or of assisting another individual or an enterprise in doing so.

(b) Businesses, whose primary purpose is not growth, manufacture, sale, 
import/export, or distribution of marijuana or THC products and other 
related items, such as bookstores, cafes, bakeries, clothing stores, gas 
stations, etc.

b. Marijuana Business. A Marijuana Business means any individual, partnership, 
corporation, association, issuer, or other legal entity, and any union or group of 
individuals associated in fact although not a legal entity, which is engaged in 
manufacture, distribution, import/export to/from the United States, of marijuana, as 
defined by the CSA (21 U.S.C. § 802 et seq), and/or marijuana related items. 
Owner. An owner of a Marijuana Business is a person or entity that controls, or 
has a legal right to control, use, manage, and convey, a Marijuana Business, 
regardless of the number of owned shares, or its legal status. 
Operator. An operator of a Marijuana Business means a person or entity that 
conducts the day-to-day operations of the Marijuana Business. 
Vendor. A vendor at a Marijuana Business means a person or entity that 
intentionally makes sales of, intentionally delivers, distributes, or dispenses, or 
offers or attempts to deliver, distribute, or dispense, marijuana and/or its related 
items at retail from a permanent or movable stand, or from an Internet site, 
whether in the United States or abroad, for the purpose of making profits. 
Knowing Participation. Knowing participation in or close association with Marijuana 
Businesses, including assisting or encouraging these commercial enterprises, is also a 
violation of the UCMJ. Coast Guard members who knowingly associate with Marijuana 
Businesses, or persons engaged with such businesses, to the extent of and under 
circumstances that discredit the service or are prejudicial to good order and discipline, 
may be punishable under Article 134, UCMJ. 
Prohibition on Entry Into and Support of Marijuana Establishments. Coast Guard military 
personnel are prohibited from knowingly visiting, entering, remaining in, patronizing, or 
otherwise conducting any kind of business with any Marijuana Businesses.

a. This prohibition applies to fixed locations, mobile dispensaries, and online or delivery 
services, whether in the United States or abroad.

b. This prohibition does not extend to: 
Medical facilities or pharmacies distributing U.S. FDA-approved prescription 
7-4

medications containing THC or CBD. 
Businesses, whose primary purpose is not growth, manufacture, sale or 
distribution of marijuana or THC products and other related items, such as 
bookstores, cafes, bakeries, clothing stores, gas stations, etc.

section: Chapter 8 -- Prohibition on Possession of Drug Paraphernalia
chapter_title: Prohibition on Possession of Drug Paraphernalia
heading_path: Prohibition on Possession of Drug Paraphernalia
text:
A. Objective. Substance abuse by members of the Coast Guard is incompatible with military 
standards of discipline, performance, and operational readiness. It is the goal of the Coast 
Guard to eliminate the wrongful possession, use, promotion or distribution of drug 
paraphernalia.

B. Policy. The sale, distribution, import into, or export out of the customs territory of the United 
States of drug paraphernalia by Coast Guard military members is prohibited by 21 U.S.C. § 
863 and this chapter. Further, Coast Guard members will not wrongfully use, possess, or 
manufacture drug paraphernalia as defined by 21 U.S.C. § 863. Members with a bona fide 
medical or clinical purpose, or as required for the performance of their duties, are exempt 
from this requirement. Coast Guard members will also not introduce drug paraphernalia onto 
a federal installation, vessel, vehicle, or aircraft; unless the member has a bona fide medical 
or clinical prupose, or it is required for the performance of their duties. This policy constitutes 
a lawful punitive order the violation of which is punishable under Article 92 and Article 134, 
UCMJ. Violations of this policy also constitute a DI. Members found in violation must be 
processed for separation in accordance with Reference (b), as appropriate.

C. Drug Paraphernalia. Drug paraphernalia is defined under 21 U.S.C. § 863 and includes but 
is not limited to equipment, products, and materials of any kind that are used to traffic, 
supply, inject, ingest, inhale, cultivate, manufacture, or otherwise introduce into the human 
body any drug or substance in order to abuse it.

D. Matters Considered in the Determination of What Constitutes Drug Paraphernalia. To 
ensure that innocently possessed objects are not classified as drug paraphernalia, the criminal 
intent of the person in possession or control of an object is a key element of the drug 
paraphernalia definition. Section 863 of Title 21, U.S.C, contains matters for consideration in 
determining whether an item is drug paraphernalia. Some additional factors to consider in 
determining criminal intent include: 
Statements. Statements by the person in possession of the object concerning its use; 
Proximity to Drug/Controlled Substances. The proximity of the object, in time and space, 
to the unlawful use, possession, or distribution of drugs/controlled substances; 
Proximity to Prohitibed Substances. The proximity of the object to prohibited 
substances; 
Residue. The existence of residue of prohibited/controlled substances on the object; 
Instructions. Instructions, oral or written, provided with the object concerning its use; 
8-1

Descriptions. Descriptive materials accompanying the object which explain or depict its 
use; 
Scope of Legitimate Use. The existence and scope of legitimate uses for the object in the 
community; and, 
Testimony. Expert testimony concerning its use.

source: CG Military Substance Abuse and Behavioral Addiction Program (COMDTINST 1000.10B)
text:
A. Gambling Disorder. 
Objective. Gambling Disorder, as a Behavioral Addiction, is treated the same as alcohol or 
drugs from a medicinal standpoint. Gambling Disorder is known to co-occur with anxiety, 
depression, and the substance use disorders described within this Instruction. A gambling 
disorder referral must be made in accordance with Reference (a). 
Definition. Gambling Disorder is defined in Reference (j) as a p ersistent and recurrent 
problematic gambling behavior leading to clinically significant impairment or distress, as 
indicated by the individual exhibiting four (or more) of the following in a 12-month period: 
a. A need to gamble with increasing amounts of money in order to achieve the desired 
excitement; 
b. Is restless or irritable when attempting to cut down or stop gambling; 
c. Has made repeated unsuccessful efforts to control, cut back, or stop gambling; 
d. Is often preoccupied with gambling (e.g., having persistent thoughts of reliving past 
gambling experiences, handicapping or planning the next venture, thinking of ways to 
get money with which to gamble); 
e. Often gambles when feeling distressed (e.g., helpless, guilty, anxious, depressed); 
f. After losing money gambling, often returns another day to get even (“chasing” one’s 
losses); 
g. Lies to conceal the extent of involvement with gambling; 
h. Has jeopardized or lost a significant relationship, job, or educational or career 
opportunity because of gambling; and, 
i. Relies on others to provide money to relieve desperate financial situations caused by 
gambling. 
Policy. The Coast Guard will identify and provide appropriate treatment and counseling to 
military members afflicted with a gambling disorder to help mitigate and prevent individual 
readiness issues. 
Commanding Officers (COs) and Officers-in-Charge (OICs). 
a. COs/OICs who suspect a member may have a gambling problem should refer that 
member for a medical evaluation of a potentially addictive disorder. 
9-1

b. COs/OICs should encourage members and their families to use CG-SUPRT 
counseling and Personal Financial Management (PFM) resources to both proactively 
address individual and familial concerns and establish healthy financial management 
practices.

B. Addictive Behaviors. Addictive behavior may interfere with a member’s day-to-day life and 
cause substantial harm and adverse consequences, both personally and professionally. 
Commands should endeavor to recognize signs that a member may have a behavioral 
addiction and seek guidance from medical providers to maximize opportunities for early 
intervention. If a member’s addictive behavior becomes disruptive or detracts from 
operational effectiveness, commands should seek guidance from their servicing legal office as 
well as from medical providers. Members who have trouble with addictive behaviors are 
unlikely to regulate those behaviors by themselves. 
9-2
